Title: A novel and efficient feature extraction algorithm using kmer-derived mutation signal
Key summary: Identified a specific k-mer “anchor” sequence that appears exclusively in BDBV genomes, suggesting it may represent a mutation pattern unique to this virus type. This computational feature extraction method used these “anchors” to distinguish viral types and support phylogenetic analysis, showing that BDBV shares some mutation pattern similarities with other filoviruses while also possessing distinctive genomic signatures.
Outcome Summary: MUTATIONS:Identified a specific k-mer “anchor” sequence (notably CGCG) that appears exclusively in Bundibugyo (BDBV) genomes, suggesting it may represent a mutation pattern unique to this virus type. This computational feature extraction method used these “anchors” to distinguish viral types and support phylogenetic analysis, showing that BDBV shares some mutation pattern similarities with other filoviruses while also possessing distinctive genomic signatures.
Methods Summary: The study developed a computational k-mer–based feature extraction algorithm that identifies biologically meaningful “anchor” sequences by analyzing reverse, complementary, and reverse-complementary k-mers to model mutation patterns across viral genomes, including Bundibugyo virus. It then quantified these anchors using a weighted interval entropy measure and evaluated the method.
Citation: (A novel and efficient feature extraction algorithm using kmer-derived mutation signal, 2026)

Title: Evidence Map: Ebola (Bundibugyo): Consolidating and summarising available evidence to support the demonstration of effectiveness of developmental vaccines.
Key summary: The Australian Living Evidence Collaboration developed an on-going evidence map that  aims to provide a consolidation and summary of the available evidence to support demonstration of effectiveness of developmental vaccines for protection against priority pathogens. Key foci include clinical symptomology (acute and long-term sequalae), risk factors for severity. diagnostic tools. Details of all studies are mapped.
Foci: Acute infection characteristics; Long term infection characteristics; Mortality risk factors; Infection immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); Hybrid immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); DTA; Mental health
Population: Human - General population, Animal - model of disease
Outcome Summary:
* only key studies in humans are extracted below. Details available for human, non-human primates and animal models: 

CLINICAL DATA- ACUTE- SEQUELAE:
Survivors of BDBV exhibited persistent multisystem symptoms, with neurological and musculoskeletal complaints most frequent, headaches (35 %) and visual disturbances (22.5 %). Laboratory findings showed elevated basophils (40 %) and urinary ketones (5 %), indicating possible chronic inflammation and metabolic shifts. Respiratory rates were significantly reduced in survivors (p < 0.001), while other vital signs and biochemical markers were largely within normal ranges. Despite high resilience, 70 % with normal anxiety scores and 62.5 % with normal depression scores, 57.5 % reported persistent stigma.

CLINICAL DATA - LONG-TERM SEQUELAE:
Survivors of the Bundibugyo Ebola virus were at significantly increased risk of ocular deficits (retro-orbital pain [RR 4·3, 95% CI 1·9-9·6; p<0·0001], blurred vision [1·9, 1·1-3·2; p=0·018]), hearing loss (2·3, 1·2-4·5; p=0·010), difficulty swallowing (2·1, 1·1-3·9; p=0·017), difficulty sleeping (1·9, 1·3-2·8; p=0·001), arthralgias (2·0, 1·1-3·6; p=0·020), and various constitutional symptoms controlling for age and sex. Chronic health problems (prevalence ratio [PR] 2·1, 95% CI 1·2-3·6; p=0·008) and limitations due to memory loss or confusion (PR 5·8, 1·5-22·4; p=0·010) were also reported more frequently by survivors of Bundibugyo Ebola virus.

RISK FACTORS- MORTALITY:
Correlation between fatal infection and immune response in BDBV in 44 patient sample found that fatal infection was associated with high levels of viral antigen, low levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as IL-1?, IL-1?, IL-6, TNF-?, and high levels of immunosuppressor cytokines like IL-10. 

IMMUNITY:
Neutralization of antibodies and immune response is categorized and mapped for both previous infection and vaccination from Ebola virus vaccine. 

DIAGNOSTIC TEST ACCRUARY:
Evidence gap map breaks out the different assays used for infection and outcome. Specifically, ELISA, Microneutralization, FRNT/PRNT, Live virus, pseudovirus, Other neutralising assay, ELISPOT, Intracellular cytokine staining, other Tcell assays.
Methods Summary:
An Evidence (gap) Map is a systematic evidence curation tool that displays all the available evidence relevant to a specific research question or topic of interest as a matrix. Bundibugyo virus map 18 May 2026 and end date search 29 May 2026 with consistent updates. 

Questions: What tools and evidence exist, and where are the evidence gaps, in measures of immune response and immunity for MARV, SUDV and EBOV?*  What is an appropriate typology/framework for comprehensive classification of tools and evidence in preclinical vaccine assessment?


The study will address this question by identifying and organising the evidence across the following domains for all pathogens:

-Immunoassay landscape 
-Immune responses and immune protection following infection and vaccination 
-Animal models developed for use in testing vaccine and mAbs candidates 
-Safety, immunogenicity and efficacy data from animal challenge studies
-Clinical safety and immunogenicity data following administration of vaccine and mAbs candidates


PubMed, Embase, Europe PMC.

Reference: Akilimali, et al, (2026), The New England journal of medicine
Title: Clinical Characteristics of Patients Infected with Bundibugyo Virus, DRC 2026
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Observational, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: This retrospective cohort study analyzed 2,351 recorded cases of suspected  Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD), finding 505 laboratory-confirmed cases (21.5%) and 635 negative tests (27.0%), with confirmed cases showing higher mortality than those that tested negative (18.8% vs. 10.2%).  Demographic and symptom profiles were similar across groups, with confirmed cases more likely to present with fever, gastrointestinal symptoms, and systemic signs than those that tested negative.  Among 129 confirmed cases , a 7.4-day mean delay between symptom onset and sample collection was observed and among 253 confirmed cases, patients who died had lower cycle-threshold (Ct) values, indicating a higher viral load, than those with no recorded death (median Ct value, 24.4 vs. 28.7; mean difference in Ct value, ?3.7.
Foci: Other Epi; Acute infection characteristics; Other clinical data
Outcome Summary:
CLINICAL DATA Acute infection: 
Patients with confirmed BVD frequently presented with fever (74.3%), gastrointestinal symptoms (vomiting 66.7%, diarrhea 67.4%), and systemic signs like headache (42.2%) and difficulty breathing (34.1%), while hemorrhagic signs were rare (10.4%).  

In confirmed cases, the symptom profile varied little according to patient sex or age after mutual adjustment. Fever was somewhat less frequent in male patients than in female patients (adjusted risk ratio, 0.88; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.78 to 1.00), and headache became less frequent with increasing age (adjusted risk ratio, 0.89 per 10-year increment; 95% CI, 0.83 to 0.96). Cough was modestly more common in male patients than in female patients (adjusted risk ratio, 1.89; 95% CI, 1.01 to 3.53), as was chest pain (adjusted risk ratio, 1.94; 95% CI, 1.01 to 3.71), whereas the relative risk of other signs and symptoms did not vary substantially according to patient sex or age. 

The signs-and-symptoms profile distinguished confirmed BVD cases from negative cases, with confirmed patients more likely to report vomiting (66.7% vs. 50.8%), diarrhea (67.4% vs. 49.6%), anorexia (39.5% vs. 17.2%), abdominal pain (37.3% vs. 16.3%), difficulty breathing (34.1% vs. 10.9%), and difficulty swallowing (32.8% vs. 10.7%); fever was also more frequent in confirmed cases (74.3% vs. 64.9%), while hemorrhagic signs showed no substantial difference (10.4% vs. 10.3%).

CLINICAL DATA OTHER:
Patients with confirmed BVD were demographically similar to those who tested negative; in both groups, the majority of patients were adults 20 and 39 years of age, and there were slightly more female patients than male patients.

RISK FACTORS OF MORTALITY :
Among 253 patients with laboratory-confirmed positive samples tested with the RADIONE PCR assay2 from the laboratory in Bunia, Ituri Province, DRC (as of June 5, 2026), patients who died had lower cycle-threshold (Ct) values, indicating a higher viral load, than those with no recorded death (median Ct value, 24.4 [interquartile range, 21.3 to 26.9] vs. 28.7 [interquartile range, 25.0 to 32.5]; mean difference in Ct value, ?3.7 [95% CI, ?5.2 to ?2.3]).

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL PARAMETER - OTHER:
Among 129 patients who tested positive for whom the symptom-onset and sample dates were recorded, the mean delay, with accounting for right truncation and censoring was 7.4 days (95% credible interval, 5.3 to 13.5; median, 4.8 days [95% credible interval, 3.5 to 7.8]). The 7.4-day mean delay between symptom onset and the date on which samples were obtained may reflect a lack of diagnostic capacity early during the outbreak.
Methods Summary:
We report here signs and symptoms and basic laboratory data from the current 2026 BVD outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). 

Among 2351 recorded cases in the individual-level database for BVD between May 3 and June 8, 2026, a total of 505 patients (21.5%) had a laboratory-confirmed case of BVD (as determined by Altona Diagnostics and RADIONE polymerase-chain-reaction [PCR] tests2), and 635 patients (27.0%) with suspected BVD tested negative. The remaining patients were untested, had pending results, or had results that remained classified as being suspected or probable. Among the 505 patients with laboratory-confirmed BVD who had records in the database, 95 (18.8%) had died by the data-cutoff date, as compared with 65 of the 635 patients (10.2%) who had tested negative. This finding cannot be interpreted as the case fatality rate owing to limited data on final outcome and unknown denominators.
Citation: Akilimali, P., Ebengo, D. M., Scarpino, S. V., Amuri-Aziza, A., Wawina-Bokalanga, T., Matondo-Mbundu, P., . . . Mbala-Kingebeni, P. (2026). Clinical characteristics of patients infected with bundibugyo virus, DRC 2026. The New England Journal of Medicine, doi:10.1056/nejmc2608070

Title: Development of an immunochromatographic lateral flow assay for the rapid detection of all known orthoebolavirus glycoproteins
Key summary: This study developed a prototype immunochromatographic lateral flow rapid diagnostic test (ILF -RDT ) that detects the glycoproteins (GPs) from all known orthoebolaviruses, including Bundibugyo virus (BDBV). The ILF-RDT detects both recombinant GP and authentic virus in samples spiked with human blood and experimentally infected animals and non-human primate samples at 7 days post-infection for BDBV, before animals exhibited severe disease. The ILF -RDT detects Ebola virus (EBOV), Sudan virus (SUDV), and BDBV GPs, both EBOV and BDBV GP produced positive results down to 31.2 ng, while SUDV GP was more difficult to interpret, with positive results at 500 and 250 ng but equivocal results at lower amounts of recombinant protein . The ILF -RDT was unable to detect GP from the more distantly related filovirus, Marburg virus, indicating that the test is specific for orthoebolaviruses but does not replace more sensitive diagnostic tests such as RT-qPCR.
Population: Human - General population, Animal - model of disease
Outcome Summary:
Diagnostic test accuracy: 
Both EBOV and SUDV (secreted glycoprotein) sGP were detected in this assay, with SUDV sGP producing particularly robust positive results at every dilution tested. After evaluating the performance of the prototype ILF -RDT in the presence of human plasma or whole blood, the RDT detected EBOV GP, EBOV sGP, SUDV sGP, and BDBV GP at amounts as low as 62.5 or 31.2 ng. SUDV GP was clearly detected down to 250 ng, with equivocal results at lower amounts. To determine whether the test could detect authentic virus, SUDV and BDBV all produced clear positive results. The authors also evaluated the assay using whole blood samples obtained from experimentally infected ferrets and macaques and the EBOV-infected ferrets showed clear positive results at 5 days post -infection (DPI), corresponding to a Ct value of ~9, when the animals were severely ill while BDBV-infected ferrets were positive when they tested at 7 days post-infection DPI with CT values around 8 for BDBV. In macaques, 8 of 10 animals, remained positive for EBOV at 7 DPI (with Ct values ~19 -22), with two other animals (#3 and #7) showing very faint bands at the test line. 

While EBOV variants Makona -C07, Kikwit, and Mayinga all produced positive results, Makona -C05 did not. GP sequence analysis revealed only a single amino acid difference between Makona -C07 and Makona -C05, namely a tryptophan to arginine mutation at position 291 (W291R) (Fig. S4B). Importantly, this mutation is rare: analysis of all 2,985 available orthoebolavirus GP sequences in GenBank revealed that only 14 possessed the W291R substitution, with all 14 belonging exclusively to sequences of EBOV variant Makona -C05. A subsequent ELISA demonstrated that monoclonal antibodies (mAb) 5A3, unlike 6H8, did not bind to the GP from Makona -C05, suggesting that the W291R point mutation specifically obstructs binding to the assay ’s capture mAb.
Methods Summary: The researchers developed an immunochromatographic lateral flow assay (ILF-RDT) by generating orthoebolavirus-specific monoclonal antibodies, conjugating detection antibodies to gold nanoparticles, and spraying capture antibodies onto nitrocellulose membranes. Test strips were assembled with laminated pads, and samples (recombinant proteins, authentic viruses, and infected animal blood) were tested using a specialized running buffer. The assay was validated for specificity and sensitivity, with results confirmed via RT-qPCR and protein sequence analysis.
Citation: Banadyga, L., Cao, W., Chan, M., Emeterio, K., Liu, G., Schulz, H., . . . Yang, M.Development of an immunochromatographic lateral flow assay for the rapid detection of all known orthoebolavirus glycoproteins. Npj Viruses, doi:https://doi.org/10.1038/s44298-026-00209-1

Reference: Bhadelia, et al, (2026), International journal of infectious diseases : IJID : official publication of the International Society for Infectious Diseases
Title: Regional Signals Preceding the 2026 Bundibugyo Virus Disease Outbreak
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Descriptive, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: The Biothreats Emergence, Analysis, and Communications Network (BEACON)  detected four temporally proximal VHF-compatible illness signals: (1) March 9, North Kivu Province—suspected Ebola case under investigation with unresolved laboratory results; (2) March 10, Kasaď Province—fatal hemorrhagic illness with secondary cases and negative Ebola PCR; (3) March 30, Burundi—35-case undiagnosed cluster near the DRC border with 5 deaths, negative testing for major filoviruses and >200 pathogens, pending metagenomic sequencing; (4) April 22, South Sudan—three suspected VHF cases with negative initial testing. All four signals shared a similar diagnostic phenotype: VHF-compatible presentation, mobilization of investigation teams, negative initial testing, and no publicly reported confirmed etiology
Outcome Summary: EPI PARAMETER- Cluster investigation: BEACON detected four temporally proximal VHF-compatible illness signals: (1) March 9, North Kivu Province—suspected Ebola case under investigation with unresolved laboratory results; (2) March 10, Kasaď Province—fatal hemorrhagic illness with secondary cases and negative Ebola PCR; (3) March 30, Burundi—35-case undiagnosed cluster near the DRC border with 5 deaths, negative testing for major filoviruses and >200 pathogens, pending metagenomic sequencing; (4) April 22, South Sudan—three suspected VHF cases with negative initial testing. All four signals shared a similar diagnostic phenotype: VHF-compatible presentation, mobilization of investigation teams, negative initial testing, and no publicly reported confirmed etiology. None were formally reported to have been resolved. The detection of four unresolved VHF signals preceding the confirmed BDBV outbreak highlights gaps in formal follow-up mechanisms for negative cases and fragmented regional diagnostic coordination.
Methods Summary: The regional surveillance signals detected by BEACON, an open access event based surveillance program, in the weeks preceding outbreak declaration are described. Authors reviewed BEACON reports of VHF-compatible illness clusters detected in the transboundary DRC-Uganda-Burundi-South Sudan region during March–April 2026, prior to the May 15 laboratory confirmation of BDBV.

Title: Mapping the Global Evidence Base of Bundibugyo Ebolavirus Disease: A Systematic Scoping Review of Research Gaps and Preparedness Priorities
Key summary: Only 23% of included studies specifically examined Bundibugyo virus disease (BDBV), with most research focused on other ebolaviruses, particularly Zaire ebolavirus, and concentrated on epidemiological drivers and clinical features. Major gaps remain in response-related research, including the absence of licensed BDBV-specific vaccines, therapeutics, and validated rapid diagnostics, while survivor outcomes are poorly characterized and study quality is limited by moderate risk of bias from missing outcome data and selective reporting.
Foci: Incidence / prevalence; Long term infection characteristics; Mortality; Vaccination immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Infection immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); Thearapeutics; DTA; Pathogen mutations; Phylogenetic analysis/molecular epidemiology
Population: Human - General population, Animal - natural infection
Outcome Summary: Only 23% of the included studies specifically addressed BDBV, with the remainder focusing on broader ebolavirus topics dominated by Zaire ebolavirus (EBOV). Applying the conceptual model revealed pronounced asymmetries across the research domains: the literature remains heavily concentrated within upstream epidemiological drivers and clinical features. Conversely, critical health system response domains lack validated tools; no licensed vaccine, specific therapeutic agent, or point-of-care rapid diagnostic test validated for BDBV currently exists. While cross-reactive monoclonal antibodies show preclinical efficacy, clinical-grade validation remains absent, and longitudinal survivor outcomes are sparsely documented. Systemic moderators, particularly weak health infrastructure and conflict, continue to compound these gaps. Methodological risk of bias was moderate, driven by missing outcome data (42% of studies) and selective reporting (41%). Conclusions BDBV remains understudied relative to its epidemic potential.
Methods Summary: This systematic scoping review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses Extension for Scoping Reviews (PRISMA-ScR) guidelines. The protocol was prospectively registered on the Open Science Framework (available at: https://osf.io/q8r63). Searches were executed programmatically using a custom Python 3.12 script on 1 May 2026 across PubMed/MEDLINE, OpenAlex, and Semantic Scholar, supplemented by manual searches of Scopus, Web of Science, WHO Disease Outbreak News, Africa CDC, medRxiv, and ReliefWeb. The PubMed strategy combined MeSH terms and free-text synonyms across 24 topic-specific queries.
Citation: Boatemaa, A., Osei, B., & Forkuo, G. O. (2026). Mapping the global evidence base of bundibugyo ebolavirus disease: A systematic scoping review of research gaps and preparedness priorities. Research Square, doi:10.21203/rs.3.rs-9948439/v1

Title: Size of the 2026 Ebola outbreak and risk of cross-border spillover from Bundibugyo virus in Ituri Province, DR Congo, and its implications for preparedness: a recalibrated stochastic modelling study
Key summary: This study aimed to establish a short-term trajectory of the BDBV outbreak and probability of cross-border spillover into countries with elevated risk of importation to guide preparedness priorities. Aligning with the central scenario (median R? = 1.71), the current outbreak is projected to have 990 cumulative confirmed cases and 174 deaths by Week 12, and cross-border spillover risks are highest for Uganda (94.2% importation probability) and South Sudan (69.3%) , requiring urgent preparedness.
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration:
Central transmissibility scenario (mean accepted R0=1·71): The ensemble projected a median of 648 cumulative confirmed cases by week 10 (June 8, 2026; 50% prediction interval [PI] 560–732), rising to 990 cases by week 12 (June 24, 2026: 50% PI 858–1141 and 90% PI 709–1293), with a projected median of 174 deaths at week 12. 

Low transmissibility scenario  (mean accepted R0=1·42): Cumulative confirmed cases are projected to reach a median of 870 by week 12 (50% PI 752–1004 and 90% PI 641–1133; 160 deaths) and 3662 by week 24 (50% PI 2884–4484 and 90% PI 2094–6037) with 673 deaths

High transmissibility scenario (mean accepted R0=2·08): confirmed cases projected to reach a median of 1364 (50% PI 1154–1579 and 90% PI 975–1807; 202 deaths) by week 12, rising to 66?420 (50% PI 56?560–78?937 and 90% PI 41?718–95?881; 10?644 deaths) by week 24. The observed confirmed-case trajectory is consistent with the central scenario. 

The model predicts very high cross-border spillover risk for Uganda (94.2% importation probability, 19 confirmed cases as of June 4) and elevated risk for South Sudan (69.3%, driven by porous borders and sustained transmission in DR Congo), while Rwanda and Burundi face low risk (8.6% and 2.0%, respectively). Uganda’s outbreak is already established with local transmission, and South Sudan’s risk remains substantial despite DR Congo’s trajectory aligning with lower scenarios.
Methods Summary:
The study used a stochastic SEIRD model (susceptible, exposed, infectious, recovered, dead) calibrated to laboratory-confirmed Bundibugyo virus cases in DR Congo (598 cases as of June 8, 2026) to project outbreak trajectories under low, central, and high transmissibility scenarios. A linked daily-hazard spillover model estimated cross-border importation risks for Uganda, South Sudan, Rwanda, and Burundi over 12 weeks, incorporating mobility, detection sensitivity, and travel-while-infectious parameters. Sensitivity analyses tested assumptions for travel, reporting completeness, and catchment size.

Case data were taken from the WHO AFRO External Situation Reports and the WHO AFRO internal tracker through to June 8, 2026. The laboratory-confirmed case series was used as the primary calibration metric: confirmed cases rose from 33 on May 18, 2026, to 105 on May 24, 2026, and 598 on June 8, 2026.15 Laboratory-confirmed cases were preferred over suspected cases because they are specific to BDBV and not subject to the over-ascertainment affecting suspected counts.
Citation: Chamla, D., Belizaire, M. R. D., Co, I. F., Jinadu, A., Mamadu, I., & Atagbaza, A. O. (2026). Size of the 2026 ebola outbreak and risk of cross-border spillover from bundibugyo virus in ituri province, DR congo, and its implications for preparedness: A recalibrated stochastic modelling study. The Lancet.Infectious Diseases, , S1473â€“8. doi:10.1016/s1473-3099(26)00320-8

Title: Risk of Cross-Border Spillover from the 2026 Bundibugyo Ebolavirus Outbreak in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of the Congo and Its Implications on Preparedness: A Stochastic Modelling Study
Key summary: Under the central transmissibility scenario, cumulative reported cases reached a median of 229 by week 12 (90% prediction interval 143–358), with a projected median of 77 deaths. Uganda had the highest probability of importation (79.9%), consistent with the subsequently confirmed imported case. South Sudan had a projected importation probability of 37·5%, whereas Rwanda (3·0%) and Burundi (0·4%) remained at low risk.
Foci: Size or Duration estimate; Mortality; Border measure - PHM
Outcome Summary:
Epi parameters - outbreak size & Clinical data - mortality: Under the central transmissibility scenario, cumulative reported cases reached a median of 229 by week 12 (90% prediction interval 143-358), with a projected median of 77 deaths. Uganda had the highest probability of importation (79.9%), consistent with the subsequently confirmed imported case. South Sudan had a projected importation probability of 37.5%, whereas Rwanda (3.0%) and Burundi (0.4%) remained at low risk. 
PHM - Border measures:  Although Bundibugyo ebolavirus is associated with lower transmissibility and mortality than Zaire ebolavirus, the prolonged pre-detection period and extensive regional population mobility create substantial risk of cross-border dissemination. Preparedness interventions should prioritise Uganda and South Sudan to mitigate potential spread and strengthen regional containment capacity.
Methods Summary: Developed a stochastic susceptible-exposed-infectious-recovered-dead (SEIRD) ensemble model calibrated to observed reported case counts and parameterised using historical Bundibugyo ebolavirus transmission characteristics. Three transmissibility scenarios were modelled over a 12-week horizon. A linked spillover model estimated the probability of importation into neighbouring countries using mobility volumes, infectious prevalence, and residual undetected border-crossing assumptions.

Title: Simulation of safe and dignified burial for control of Bundibugyo Ebola virus epidemics

Key summary: Safe and dignified burial (SDB) significantly reduces Ebola transmission by lowering the reproduction number (R), with high coverage and effectiveness being critical to achieving epidemic control or extinction, particularly under low transmissibility conditions. The longer it takes for SDB to get underway, the harder it will take to bring outbreaks under control and it may be efficient to preposition and activate SDB even in areas where no transmission is yet observed which may prevent geographic spillover.

Public Health Measures - other: The combination of highly performant SDB and isolation and case management would probably make a formidable contribution to transmission. Moreover, SDB as a trust-building measure is likely to encourage care-seeking and case reporting, and could thus act synergistically with other interventions.

Title: Remdesivir triphosphate as a potential repurposed drug against the emerging Bundibugyo Ebolavirus 2026
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Experimental, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: Based on the mutation mapping profile, the authors observed no new mutations in the RdRp catalyist domain and the binding affinity of RTP to BDBV 2026 RdRp was found similar to the binding affinity of RTP to SARS-CoV-2 RdRp catalytic domain. RTP was found to tightly fit within the pocket site and the complex of the RTP and RdRp showed dense molecular interactions aiding in stabilizing the complex.
Foci: Pathogen mutations; pathogen structural characterization; pathogen  Protein-inhibitor/binding interactions
Outcome Summary: PATHOGEN STUDIES - Mutations, Structural characterization, and Protein-inhibitor/binding interactions: The authors mapped the mutations in the L protein encoded by the newly sequenced genome of BDBV 2026 from the ongoing ebola outbreak in DRC and Uganda. Based on the mutation mapping profile, they observed no new mutations in the RdRp catalyist domain. Subsequently, they modeled the RdRp BDBV 2026 protein and docked RTP with the RdRp catalyist domain. RTP is an approved antiviral drug for SARS-CoV-2. The binding affinity of RTP to BDBV 2026 RdRp was found similar to the binding affinity of RTP to SARS-CoV-2 RdRp catalytic domain. RTP was found to tightly fit within the pocket site and the complex of the RTP and RdRp showed dense molecular interactions aiding in stabilizing the complex.
Methods Summary: The authors performed molecular and mutational annotation of RNA-dependent RNA polymerase from BDBV and molecular docking of Remdesivir triphosphate (RTP) to RNA-dependent RNA polymerase in BDBV to evaluate inhibition potential inhibition of RTP against the RdRp encoded by the Large (L) protein of the BDBV. They sequenced a clinical isolate (CL023114) of a 59-year-old BDBV infected patient which was identified as travel case. Genome sequencing was performed using the Illumina NextSeq and the sequence was submitted to the Pathoplexus platform with accession number of PP_006XCJJ.1. To predict an accurate three dimensional (3D) structural model of the L protein (RdRp), the Google DeepMind-based AlphaFold3 algorithm was employed.
Citation: Dutt, M., Wayengera, M., Martinez, G. S., Kumar, A., Nsawotebba, A., Ssewanya, I., . . . Sabiiti, W. (2026). Remdesivir triphosphate as a potential repurposed drug against the emerging bundibugyo ebolavirus 2026. The Journal of Infection, 93(2), 106788. doi:10.1016/j.jinf.2026.106788

Title: Threat assessment brief: Ebola disease outbreak caused by Bundibugyo virus – Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda – 2026
Key summary:
The likelihood of secondary transmission of BDBV within the EU/EEA and the occurrence of sustained chains of transmission within the EU/EEA is considered very low, as cases are likely to be promptly identified and isolated and recommended control measures would be implemented. Although BDBV infection can cause severe disease in affected individuals, the population-level public health impact in the EU/EEA is expected to be very low because only very few cases would occur. Therefore, the overall current risk of BDBV for the general population in the EU/EEA is assessed to be very low.
Recommendations for standard operating procedures on isolation are also provided.

Title: Estimation of the importation risk of Bundibugyo virus into the EU/EEA in June 2026
Key summary: Based on mathematical modelling, the authors estimate approximately one importation of  Bundibugyo virus (BDBV) per 24 000 travellers (90% Uncertainty Interval, UI: 13 000–54 000) from the main outbreak region (North Kivu and Ituri, DRC) to the EU/EEA.  The authors also estimate the probability of at least one BDBV importation into the EU/EEA from 11–25 June 2026 to be 0.45% (90% UI: 0.20%–0.85%), under the hypothetical assumption that 100 people travel from the outbreak region to the EU/EEA during this period.
Outcome Summary: Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration: While sporadic BDBV importations into the EU/EEA cannot be ruled out, mathematical modelling suggests that the probability of importation from 11 to 25 June is very low. These results apply to importation of BDBV from the general population of Ituri and North Kivu. Humanitarian aid workers or healthcare care personnel returning from the outbreak region to the EU/EEA, who the authors assume would be medically evacuated from the affected areas with application of appropriate infection prevention and control measures, need to be considered separately. As one BDBV importation is expected per 24 000 travellers from the outbreak region, the vast majority of travellers will not be infected. However, since early symptoms of BDBV infections overlap with many other conditions, a potentially large number of travellers will show similar symptoms as BDBV infections without being infected with BDBV (i.e. false positives).
Methods Summary: The authors used a two-step approach to estimate the risk of importation of BDBV into the EU/EEA. First, they developed a compartmental model for the BDBV transmission. The model concentrates in the Ituri and North Kivu provinces in DRC, where most BDBV infections have been reported from. The study period was defined as 11–25 June 2026. The estimated population size of the outbreak region is ?? = 13,392,200. The mathematical model in the first step partitions the population of the outbreak region into six compartments or disease states: those not infected (susceptible); those who are exposed, which we defined as those who are infected but not yet infectious (latently infected); those who are infectious and will recover; those who are infectious and will die; those who recovered; and the deceased. In the second step, the authors build on the output of the mathematical model to estimate the probability of at least one BDBV importation into the EU/EEA.
Citation: ECDC. (2026d). Estimation of the importation risk of bundibugyo virus into the EU/EEA in june 2026. Retrieved from https://www.ecdc.europa.eu/en/publications-data/estimation-importation-risk-bundibugyo-virus-eueea-june-2026

Title: Overview of available modelling evidence to inform the scale and potential spread of Bundibugyo virus in the current Ebola disease outbreak
Key summary: This overview assessed the modelling evidence to inform scale and spread of BDBV in DRC and Uganda. Multiple modelling groups suggest that the true size of the outbreak is larger than reported with one model estimating that cumulative infections as of 13 June were between 3.0 and 10.2 times the reported number of cases (90% credible interval). Current modelling estimates are highly uncertain due to data limitations. Relative risk of importation is highest in Rwanda, Tanzania and Kenya and low risk risk in EU/EEA.
Foci: Reproduction Number; Size or Duration estimate; Mortality
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION:
The MRC Centre for Global Infectious Disease Analysis at Imperial College London estimated that, as of 16 May 2026, the true number of infections was between 1.2 and 2.4 times the reported case count. The Epistorm group, led by Northeastern University in the United States (US) and supported by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (US CDC), estimated the size of the outbreak around the same date to be 1.16 (90% credible interval (CrI): 0.4–2.7) times larger than reported. Epiforecasts, a consortium that includes modellers from the London School of Health and Tropical Medicine, has been continuously updating its estimates of the outbreak size. On 13 June, they estimated that the true number of infections was between 3.0 and 10.2 times higher than reported (90% CrI).


Relative risk of importation (i.e. the share of risk allocated to a location) is higher for the countries with the highest connectivity to the outbreak areas, with Rwanda, Tanzania and Kenya identified as the countries with the highest relative risk. Estimates of absolute risk of importation (i.e. the probability of risk to a particular country or locality) into the EU/EEA under current prevalence levels and assuming historical travel patterns suggest only a remote chance of importation in the next two-week period. European countries with the highest connectivity to the current outbreak areas are at higher relative risk of importation; these include the UK, Belgium, Germany France and Italy.   Mid-term scenario projections from US CDC suggest a 65% probability of over 20 000 infections over the next three months under the assumption that 20% of patients are effectively isolated and no other control measures are implemented. 

MORTALITY:
Epiforecasts conducted a model fitted to multiple surveillance data streams (suspected and confirmed cases and deaths in DRC, and exported cases and deaths in Uganda) estimated a CFR 0.28 to 0.61 (90% CrI).

The WHO reported that between May 2026 to present (DRC and Uganda)  CFR is 17.4% (93 deaths of 534 cases). Other historical outbreaks reported CFR ranging from 0-88% depending on the country and outbreak.

REPRODUCTION NUMBER:
Epiforecasts conducted a model fitted to multiple surveillance data streams (suspected and confirmed cases and deaths in DRC, and exported cases and deaths in Uganda) estimated a reproduction number (R0) of 1.29 to 3.06 (90%CrI).
Methods Summary:
ECDC experts conducted a pragmatic assessment of the modelling evidence available at the time of writing. Given the rapidly evolving nature of the outbreak and the need to support situational awareness, a formal systematic or rapid review methodology was not used. The assessment drew on the authors’ participation in international modelling networks and communities of practice, discussions with subject-matter experts, engagement with modelling groups actively working on the outbreak, and monitoring of publicly available reports, preprints, publications and technical outputs relevant to the ongoing outbreak.


At this early stage of the outbreak, modelling is constrained by limited data and considerable uncertainty around key epidemiological parameters. Consequently, analyses rely on assumptions and parameter estimates derived from previous outbreaks, which may not fully reflect the current situation. Multiple combinations of parameter values can plausibly explain the available observations, meaning that a wide range of model projections may be consistent with the data. Such variation primarily reflects uncertainty about the outbreak itself rather than differences in model quality. In addition, modelling assumptions, parameter choices and the interpretation of results may be influenced by analysts’ prior expectations, methodological preferences or the framing of the questions being addressed.
Citation: ECDC. (2026e). Overview of available modelling evidence to inform the scale and potential spread of bundibugyo virus in the current ebola disease outbreak. Retrieved from https://www.ecdc.europa.eu/en/publications-data/overview-available-modelling-evidence-inform-scale-and-potential-spread

Title: Preparedness and response for imported cases of Ebola disease into an EU/EEA country
Key summary: This report presents an overview of health preparedness and response planning elements for the potential importation of a case of Ebola disease into a European Union/European Economic Area (EU/EEA) Member State health system. This document is organised into four focus areas, representing the potential health system contact points of an imported Ebola disease case. All four areas must operate effectively and in coordination with public health services to prevent further community transmission.
Outcome Summary:
Infection Prevention and Control - General workflow guidance: The four focus areas highlighted in this document include: Potential health system first contact for an unknown case of Ebola disease,  In-country transport of a probable or confirmed case of Ebola disease, Designated treatment facility for Ebola disease case(s), Medical evacuation of an Ebola disease case and Overarching public health planning elements.  For each focus area, a checklist is presented as a stand-alone table, subdivided into the organisational capabilities required to: prepare for the possibility of an imported Ebola disease case: ‘Planning, protocols and training'; recognise individuals as Persons Under Investigation (PUI) or probable cases of Ebola disease: ‘Case Recognition’; manage PUI, probable or confirmed Ebola disease patient effectively, while ensuring the safety of staff who come into contact with them: ‘Patient Management and Staff Protection’.    

Public Health Measures - Other: This document also presents an overall preparedness checklist which can serve as a template for public health planning of the response to an imported case of Ebola disease case. This checklist sets out the system elements to plan without outlining the organisational competencies in detail. System elements include: public health preparedness planning, health care infrastructure, and risk communication and community engagement – Infodemic Management (RCCEIM).
Methods Summary: This document was developed in the context of the ECDC Public Health Emergency for the Ebola outbreak 2026, drawing  upon multiple ECDC documents on public health preparedness and planning that can assist countries in their overall public health planning for such an event as well as aviation stakeholder guidance.
Citation: ECDC. (2026f). Preparedness and response for imported cases of ebola disease into an EU/EEA country. Retrieved from https://www.ecdc.europa.eu/en/publications-data/preparedness-and-response-imported-cases-ebola-disease-eueea-country

Reference: Fanelli, et al, (2026), Journal of travel medicine
Title: Estimating importation risk of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease to Europe under different outbreak expansion scenarios
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Modeling, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: This study is a published version of a preprint and it estimated the relative importation risk of Bundibugyo to Europe  through air travel under six different outbreak expansion scenarios.  Potential spread across the Ugandan border produced no change compared to the refence scenario. Expansion across the border to South Sudan produced a first structural increase in importation pressure through the connectivity associated to Juba (5-fold increase for Northern Europe and 6-fold increase for Eastern Europe relative to localized transmission in DRC). Hypothetical amplification in Kampala, Kigali, and Kinshasa substantially increased importation pressure (>120 fold and 80 -fold) and reshaped exposure patterns across Europe.
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameters - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE: 
Assessed relative exposure to Ebola virus disease importation into Europe under six outbreak expansion scenarios reflecting plausible pathways of geographical spread, including cross-border transmission and amplification in highly connected regional capitals. 


Potential spread across the Ugandan border produced no change in either the relative exposure of European countries or their overall importation pressure , compared with the reference scenario of established community transmission in Eastern DRC, with Italy, France, Belgium, and the UK being the most exposed. Expansion across the border to South Sudan produced a first structural increase in importation pressure through the connectivity associated to Juba (5-fold increase for Northern Europe and 6-fold increase for Eastern Europe relative to localized transmission in DRC), while still leaving UK, France, and Italy among the highest-risk countries. Further amplification in highly connected capitals in the region substantially altered importation patterns. Kampala and Kigali amplification increased exposure in Northern Europe, with Kampala producing a >120-fold increase in importation pressure relative to the reference scenario. Kinshasa amplification instead primarily reinforced exposure in Western Europe (~80-fold increase). In each case, the inclusion of the capital became the dominant source of importation pressure, reflecting its strong international connectivity.
Methods Summary: The study assessed Ebola importation risk to Europe using International Air Transport Association origin–destination passenger flow data from 2024. Researchers modeled six outbreak expansion scenarios, including localized transmission in eastern DRC, cross-border spread to Uganda and South Sudan, and hypothetical amplification in major urban hubs (Kampala, Kigali, Kinshasa). The relative risk of importation for each European country was normalized to the total passenger flow from the source airports to Europe.
Citation: Fanelli, F., Parino, F., Poletto, C., & Colizza, V. (2026a). Estimating importation risk of bundibugyo ebola virus disease to europe under different outbreak expansion scenarios. Journal of Travel Medicine, , taag055. doi:10.1093/jtm/taag055

Title: Interpretable Ensemble Learning for the Determinants of Ebola Outbreak Case-Fatality: A Half-Century Analysis of Orthoebolavirus Events, 1976-2026
Key summary: Researchers assembled a half-century, outbreak-level record of human orthoebolavirus events from public CDC and WHO sources and study the determinants of the case-fatality rate (CFR) with interpretable, low-variance ensemble learning under leave-one-out validation. Viral species is the dominant driver of pooled CFR near 70% for Zaire, 51% for Sudan, 24% for Bundibugyo, and outbreak scale is the second strongest feature, while contextual covariates carry a secondary signal.
Outcome Summary: Researchers assembled a half-century, outbreak-level record of human orthoebolavirus events from public CDC and WHO sources and study the determinants of the case-fatality rate (CFR) with interpretable, low-variance ensemble learning under leave-one-out validation. Viral species is the dominant driver of pooled CFR near 70% for Zaire, 51% for Sudan, 24% for Bundibugyo, and outbreak scale is the second strongest feature, while contextual covariates carry a secondary signal. Read against the 2026 Bundibugyo emergency, the results locate the current outbreak at the low-lethality end of the species spectrum while underlining that lower lethality, late species confirmation and the absence of a species-matched vaccine combine into a configuration in which scale, rather than per-case fatality, is the quantity to watch.
Methods Summary: The study uses interpretable ensemble learning (gradient boosting, random forest, elastic-net regression) with leave-one-out cross-validation to analyze Ebola outbreak CFRs from 1976–2026. It identifies viral species and outbreak scale as key predictors, supported by Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) values for feature attribution. The analysis focuses on outbreak-level data from CDC and WHO, emphasizing methodological transparency and generalizability.
Citation: Ferrara, M. (2026). Interpretable ensemble learning for the determinants of ebola outbreak case-fatality: A half-century analysis of orthoebolavirus events, 1976-2026. Research Square, doi:10.21203/rs.3.rs-9880357/v1

Title: Molecular evolutionary analysis of the current Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak in DRC and Uganda
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Modeling, Other grey literature report
Key summary:
Analysis of 10 BDBV sequences from DRC and Uganda share a most recent common ancestor between March 1 and March 15, 2026, and exhibit significant genetic diversity, indicating the outbreak had been circulating undetected for several weeks. Preliminary estimates suggest an epidemic doubling time (range: 15.2 to 24.5 days) and a reproduction number (??0) above 1 (range: 1.31 to 1.55). These findings should be interpreted cautiously due to substantial uncertainty. 
Estimates of doublings per year was determined using a generation time estimate from incubation and infectious period estimates from previous outbreak.
Foci: Reproduction Number; Size or Duration estimate; Pathogen mutations; Phylogenetic analysis/molecular epidemiology
Outcome Summary:
REPRODUCTION NUMBER:
The corresponding reproduction number (??0) estimates are centred above 1, with mean estimates ranging from 1.31 to 1.55, although substantial uncertainty remains because of the limited number of sequences currently available.  The reproduction number was estimated from the number of doublings per year using a generation time estimate derived from previous outbreak published incubation and infectious-period estimates for Bundibugyo virus disease.

OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION - DOUBLING TIME:
The estimated epidemic doubling time is relatively slow but highly uncertain. Across the six substitution-rate assumptions, the mean estimated doubling time ranges from 15.2 to 24.5 days,  although the corresponding uncertainty intervals are wide and include zero and should therefore be interpreted cautiously.

MUTATIONS & PHYLOGENETIC ANALYSIS: The sampled viruses appear to share a most recent common ancestor in early-to-mid March 2026 and are already genetically diverse. Researchers identified 23 unique mutations across the 10 sequences sampled.
Methods Summary:
Outbreak-associated BDBV sequences were downloaded from Pathoplexus and were aligned to the reference genome FJ217161.1 using MAFFT v7.490, then manually inspected in AliView. Ancestral node reconstruction was performed using IQ-TREE v3.0.1 and the best-fitting substitution model, HKY+F+I. This analysis was used to reconstruct the consensus sequence of the most recent common ancestor (MRCA) of the sampled outbreak sequences. This reconstructed MRCA sequence was then used as the root for the outbreak mutation reconstruction.

We used systematically collected Ebola virus disease parameters7 available through {epireview}8 to identify previous estimates of epidemiological delays for Bundibugyo virus disease.9,10 This gave a generation time estimate of 9.6 days. The uncertainty intervals for ??0 were obtained by propagating the 95% HPD interval from the estimated number of doublings per year.

The estimated growth rates and ??0 values are likely affected by sampling bias. The sequences included here were collected between 03 May 2026 and 20 May 2026, and therefore represent a short time window around the identification and declaration of the BVD outbreak.11 Situation reports12,13 and independent epidemiological analyses14 suggest that, even at the time these sequences were generated, the outbreak was already substantially larger than the available sequence data alone would indicate.

All analyses were performed in Delphy1 and run until the minimum effective sample size was greater than 100. The reproduction number was estimated from the number of doublings per year using a generation time estimate derived from published incubation and infectious-period estimates for Bundibugyo virus disease.7-10

Title: Longitudinal Bundibugyo Virus Glycoprotein Seroreactivity Following rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP Vaccination in Outbreak-Affected Populations of the Democratic Republic of the Congo
Key summary: This study collected serum samples between 2018-2023 from vaccine recipients in Mbandaka, Equateur Province and Beni, North Kivu Province and evaluated development of antibodies to Bundibugyo glycoprotein (GP) following rVSV?GZEBOV-GP vaccination in two EVD outbreak-affected populations. They found that baseline Bundibugyo virus disease (BDBV) GP seroreactivity differed between sites, with 3.3% of participants reactive in Mbandaka and 10.4% in Beni.  In Mbandaka, BDBV GP titers remained unchanged through 6 months post-vaccination but increased markedly between 2.5 and 3.5 years, accompanied by a rise in seroreactivity to 35.3%, followed by waning at later visits. In Beni, BDBV GP titers increased rapidly after vaccination, reaching peak seroreactivity at 21 days (52.3%) and 6 months (53.9%), with mean fold increases of 21.9 and 20.1 among baseline naďve participants.
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - SEROPREVALENCE: 
Mbandaka cohort: At baseline, 3.3% (n=16) of participants were observed to be seroreactive to BDBV GP and 28.8% (n=139) of participants were reactive to EBOV GP. No change was observed in anti-BDBV GP antibody MFI values at 21 days or 6 months postvaccination. However, there was a rise in mean BDBV GP reactivity from 1238 to 4845 MFI (p<.0001) between the year 2.5 and year 3.5 visits. Additionally, at the 3.5 year visit, 35.3% (n=116) of participants were seroreactive based on the cut-off criteria. 

Beni cohort: At baseline. reactivity was observed to be 10.4% (n=62) to BDBV GP and 3.8% (n=23) to EBOV GP. There was an immediate rise in anti-BDBV GP antibody MFI values at 21 days and 6 months after vaccination with rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP from a mean of 1653 MFI to 6678 (p<.0001). At 21 days and 6 months post-vaccination the authors observed 52.3% (n=283) and 53.9% (n=270) seroreactivity to BDBV GP, respectively. Among those seronegative to BDBV GP at baseline and seroreactive at 21 days (n=241, 40.2%) and 6 months (n= 227, 37.9%), authors recorded a mean fold change of 21.9 and 20.1 compared to baseline, respectively. Mean antibody MFI values decreased for all antigens during the 6 month to 2.5 year postvaccination period, with only those for EBOV and BDBV GP remaining above the mean MFI values at baseline.
Methods Summary:
Between 2018 and 2023, serum samples were collected from vaccine recipients in Mbandaka, Equateur Province (n=482 at baseline), and Beni, North Kivu Province (n=599 at baseline). Antibody reactivity was assessed using a multiplex pan-filovirus immunoassay. We evaluated longitudinal trends in BDBV GP seroreactivity across follow-up visits extending to approximately five years after vaccination.  


Recruitment and enrollment of this study coincided with the rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP vaccination efforts undertaken in response to the DRC’s 9th and 10th EVD outbreaks. Beginning June 2018 in Mbandaka, Equateur, and in August, 2018 in Beni, North Kivu, individuals ?1 year of age who received the rVSV?G-ZEBOV-GP vaccine under the World Health Organization (WHO) ring vaccination protocol were eligible for enrollment

Reference: Kaweesa, et al, (2026), New Microbes and New Infections
Title: Resilience and residuals beyond containment - the hidden burden of Bundibugyo Ebola virus survivorship sixteen years on: a cross-sectional observational study
Key summary: Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) survivorship was marked by persistent but heterogeneous multisystemic sequelae, particularly neurological and musculoskeletal symptoms, alongside subtle immunometabolic perturbations and remarkable mental health resilience. These findings support the need for virus-specific post-BVD care and sustained follow-up to inform survivor health policy.
Foci: Long term infection characteristics; Mental health
Outcome Summary:
CLINICAL DATA - Long-term sequelae: 
Survivors exhibited persistent multisystem symptoms, with neurological and musculoskeletal complaints most frequent, headaches (35 %) and visual disturbances (22.5 %). Laboratory findings showed elevated basophils (40 %) and urinary ketones (5 %), indicating possible chronic inflammation and metabolic shifts. Respiratory rates were significantly reduced in survivors (p < 0.001), while other vital signs and biochemical markers were largely within normal ranges. Despite high resilience, 70 % with normal anxiety scores and 62.5 % with normal depression scores, 57.5 % reported persistent stigma.
Methods Summary: Cross-sectional observational study involving 40 laboratory-confirmed BVD survivors and 23 age- and sex-matched unexposed community controls. Participants underwent comprehensive clinical evaluations, laboratory testing, and standardised mental health assessments. Statistical comparisons used rank-sum, chi-squared, and correlation analyses.

Title: Cross-protection against Bundibugyo by Ebola and Sudan vaccines
Key summary: A yellow fever 17D-vectored BDBV vaccine (YF-BDB) expressing the Bundibugyo virus (BDBV) glycoprotein (GP) as antigen conferred homologous protection in Ifnar?/? mice, preventing systemic viral dissemination and providing survival following surrogate BDBV challenge. Intriguingly, also vaccination with YF- EBO or YF-SUD protected mice from such BDBV surrogate challenge; while YF-BDB and YF-EBO fully protected against respective heterologous EBOV or BDBV infection, they conferred only partial protection against lethal challenge in a comparable Sudan virus (SUDV) model.
Foci: Vaccination immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines)
Outcome Summary: Vaccine immunity: . A yellow fever 17D-vectored BDBV vaccine (YF-BDB) expressing the BDBV glycoprotein (GP) as antigen conferred homologous protection in Ifnar?/? mice, preventing systemic viral dissemination and providing survival following surrogate BDBV challenge. Intriguingly, also vaccination with YF- EBO or YF-SUD protected mice from such BDBV surrogate challenge. Conversely, while YF-BDB and YF-EBO fully protected against respective heterologous EBOV or BDBV infection, they  conferred only partial protection against lethal challenge in a comparable SUDV model. Serological analyses revealed comparable titers of cross-reactive antibodies across all vaccine groups. However, neutralization, especially cross-neutralization, was limited suggesting a major role for non-neutralizing mechanisms in protection.
Methods Summary: Authors developed a BDBV surrogate challenge model and evaluated cross-protective immunity conferred by a set of monovalent vaccine candidates representing all three Orthoebolavirus species (EBOV, SUDV ad BDBV) affecting humans.

Title: Early assessment of potential airline-mediated importation risk during the 2026 DRC-Uganda Bundibugyo virus disease outbreak
Key summary: Effective-distance analyses identified major international hub countries, including Belgium, France, South Africa, Kenya, and the United Arab Emirates, as higher-probability gateways within 30 days, with the top-ranked countries all exceeding a probability of 0.64 (range: 0.28-0.82 in the sensitivity analysis).  These early projections provide a reproducible framework for real-time international situational awareness, while emphasizing that importation risk does not imply local transmission risk.
Outcome Summary: Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration:  The highest 30 day country-level arrival probabilities were observed in major international hub countries including Belgium, France, South Africa, Kenya, and the United Arab Emirates, with the top-ranked countries all exceeding 0.64, whose probaility ranged from 0.28 to 0.82 in the sensitivity analysis.
Methods Summary: The study projected airline-mediated importation risk for Bundibugyo virus disease during the 2026 DRC-Uganda outbreak using a hazard-based model using the 2013–2016 West African EVD epidemic. Researchers constructed a directed, weighted air-traffic network from 2024 passenger-flow data and calculated the shortest paths distances from Kinshasa/N’djili International Airport to global destinations using Dijkstra’s algorithm. Arrival probabilities were estimated along with sensitivity analyses exploring alternative starting points (Entebbe International Airport).

PROTEIN-INHIBTIOR/BINDING INTERACTIONS:
This study found that the complex MJ20, a pan-specific synthetic FAB binds strongly with the NP-C terminal domain of multiple Ebola strains including the Bundibugyo strain. The spatially studied system included the crystal structure of the complex of MJ20 with the antigen from the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola virus. The study highlights the complex and multifaceted relationships between calculated and experimental parameters for the binding of Fab branches to the Ebola (Bundibugyo) protein.
Methods Summary: We conducted a study of the co-direction of changes in thermodynamic quantities  during the transition from a monomer to a dimer and then to a trimer and tetramer. In this work, we assume that the co-direction of changes in thermodynamic quantities as the final molecular formation being achieved signals a higher affinity of molecules among themselves than there is for a biochemical formation, which is characterized by the lack of coordination of the biochemical pathway directions of the final molecular compound. As the studied molecular complexes, we took [LGP2-8dsRNA-LGP2], [VP35]2-dsRNA-[VP35]2, and MARV NPcore proteins with peptides and the complex of MJ20 with antigens from the Bundibugyo strain of Ebola virus.

Title: Rational design of next-generation filovirus vaccines combining glycoprotein stabilization and nanoparticle display with glycan modification
Key summary: The study demonstrates that self-assembling protein nanoparticles (SApNPs) displaying stabilized filovirus glycoproteins (GPs) from multiple filoviruses, including Bundibugyo virus, significantly enhance immune responses in mice, supporting their potential for broad next-generation filovirus vaccines.
Outcome Summary:
VACCINE DEVELOPMENT:
This study successfully designed and tested next-generation filovirus vaccines by stabilizing glycoproteins, optimizing nanoparticle delivery, and modifying glycans to enhance immune responses. Key outcomes included stronger antibody production, prolonged lymph node retention, and cross-reactivity against multiple filoviruses, paving the way for broadly effective vaccine development. 

For EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV, comparative analysis of GP?muc-WL2P2 and -WL2P4 constructs revealed that the P4 mutation conferred a more favorable thermostability profile, while trimer yield, purity, and antigenicity remained comparable between P2 and P4.
Methods Summary:
This study combined structural biology, protein engineering, and immunology to design filovirus vaccines by stabilizing glycoproteins, optimizing nanoparticle display, and modifying glycans. Techniques included crystal structure analysis, nanoparticle scaffolding, glycan enrichment/trimming, and in vitro/in vivo testing to assess stability, immunogenicity, and antibody responses. 
Here, we sought to obtain atomic details for the more stable EBOV GP?muc-WL2P4.
Citation: Lee Y.-Z., Zhang Y.-N., Newby M.L., Ward G., Braz Gomes K., Auclair S., . . . Zhu J. (2025). Rational design of next-generation filovirus vaccines combining glycoprotein stabilization and nanoparticle display with glycan modification. Title: Cross-reactive Bundibugyo antibody responses after licensed Ebola vaccines
Key summary: In this study, both licensed EBOV vaccines (rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo®, MSD), and Ad26.ZEBOV/MVA-BN-Filo (Zabdeno®, Mvabea®, Janssen)) induced detectable cross-reactive binding antibody responses against BDBV glycoprotein. No direct assessment of vaccine efficacy against BDBV infection or disease can be made, but the data provide biological support for the the plausibility of partial heterologous immunity against BDBV following EBOV vaccination.
Outcome Summary:
VACCINE IMMUNITY:
Detectable cross-reactive antibody responses against BDBV were observed across vaccine groups, timepoints, and age categories. However, BDBV responses remained substantially lower than homologous EBOV responses. In rVSV recipients, median BDBV responses (net MFI) reached 282 (IQR 164–644) at D28 compared with 1788 (832–3311) against the homologous Kikwit antigen. Similar patterns were observed following rVSV booster vaccination and Ad26.ZEBOV/MVA-BN-Filo vaccination. The heterologous Ad26/MVA regimen demonstrated increasing BDBV responses between D28 and M3.
Methods Summary: We assessed cross-reactive antibody responses against multiple ebolavirus species, including BDBV, using serum samples from participants enrolled in the PREVAC randomised clinical trial that evaluated the rVSV-ZEBOV (Ervebo®, MSD), and Ad26.ZEBOV/MVA-BN-Filo (Zabdeno®, Mvabea®, Janssen), vaccine strategies in adults and in children in West Africa,8 to provide urgently needed insights into potential cross-protective immunity.
Citation: Lhomme, E., Wiedemann, A., Ayouba, A., Ben-Farhat, S., Thaurignac, G., Roy, C., . . . PREVAC, s. T. (2026). Cross-reactive bundibugyo antibody responses after licensed ebola vaccines. medRxiv, , 2026.05.27.26354223. doi:10.64898/2026.05.27.26354223

Reference: McCabe, et al, (2026), The Lancet Infectious Diseases
Title: Estimation of the Ebola outbreak size in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Modeling, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: The results suggest that as of May 27, 2026, approximately 280–2520 cases of Bundibugyo virus disease might have occurred in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (estimate range 306–2521 with the back-calculation model and 282–1345 with the geographical spread model); however, there is considerable uncertainty around these estimates. The broad convergence of these estimates from two independent methods supports the conclusion of potentially substantial underdetection of cases and the potential for wider transmission.
Outcome Summary: EPI PARAMETER: Outbreak size/duration: The authors found broadly consistent results suggesting that as of May 27, 2026, approximately 280–2520 cases of Bundibugyo virus disease might have occurred in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (estimate range 306–2521 with the back-calculation model and 282–1345 with the geographical spread model); however, there is considerable uncertainty around these estimates. The broad convergence of our estimates from two independent methods supports the conclusion of potentially substantial underdetection of cases and the potential for wider transmission.
Methods Summary: To produce early estimates of the epidemic size in the Democratic Republic of the Congo as of May 27, 2026, The authors conducted scenario based analyses using two simple independent methods, with the scarce information available and making plausible assumptions about the surveillance, severity, and growth of the outbreak. The first method uses an analytical framework linking reported suspected and confirmed deaths, of which the authors assume a proportion is due to Ebola, with estimates of the case fatality ratio (33% [95% CI 26–40]; defined as the proportion of patients who die as a result of a Bundibugyo virus infection) and the time from symptom onset to death (gamma distribution mean 11·37 days [SD 5·41]) derived from previous Bundibugyo virus. The second method combined information on the three confirmed imported Bundibugyo virus disease cases reported in Uganda (as of May 27, 2026), estimates of daily international travel volumes from Ituri and Nord Kivu provinces to Uganda, assumptions about the epidemic doubling time, and we presumed that all cases in symptomatic travellers will be detected at the time of recovery or death.
Citation: McCabe, R., Ebbarnezh, L., Okware, S., Fotsing, R., Koua, E., Mbaka, P., . . . Cori, A. (2026). Estimation of the ebola outbreak size in the democratic republic of the congo. The Lancet Infectious Diseases, doi:10.1016/S1473-3099(26)00299-9

Key summary: The study evaluated immune responses to the Janssen heterologous Ebola vaccine regimen (Ad26.ZEBOV prime and MVA-BN-Filo boost) and found it induced strong EBOV glycoprotein-specific antibody and CD4 T cell responses, including binding, Fc effector, and neutralizing activity. In contrast, analysis of survivors and contacts from a Bundibugyo virus outbreak showed minimal cross-reactivity to other filoviruses, indicating that broader multivalent vaccines are needed for cross-filovirus protection.

VACCINE IMMUNITY- Measures of protective immunity (antibodies/titres/cytokines): The study evaluated immune responses to the Janssen heterologous Ebola vaccine regimen (Ad26.ZEBOV prime and MVA-BN-Filo boost) and found it induced strong EBOV glycoprotein-specific antibody and CD4 T cell responses, including binding, Fc effector, and neutralizing activity.
Methods Summary: Conducted a systems immunology analysis of antibody-mediated and cellular immune responses induced after  immunization with Janssen heterologous Ebola vaccine regimen (Ad26.ZEBOV prime and MVA-BN-Filo boost). Title: Limitations of cross-border containment strategies for Bundibugyo ebolavirus
Key summary:
The authors found that traveler screening via symptom
screening or molecular testing will miss the majority of Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) infected travelers, and should be complemented by post-arrival quarantine and monitoring of sufficient duration to detect those with long incubation periods. These findings underscore the limitations of border screening and the importance of complementary measures like post-arrival quarantine to prevent local importation of BVD.
Outcome Summary:
Public Health Measures - Border measures
A model to predict the proportion of infected travelers missed by symptom screening at arrival and post-arrival quarantine with symptom monitoring was developed. The model estimates that arrival symptom screening will miss 67.6%–86.3% of infected travelers in a steady state outbreak within the source country.  If all arriving travelers are subject to a universal post-arrival quarantine and monitoring, the researchers estimate that a 7day quarantine will miss 26.3% of infections. If both interventions are applied, the combination of arrival screening with post-arrival universal quarantine is predicted to miss 17.8%–22.7% of infected travelers.

Public Health Measures - Quarantine/isolation
Researchers conclude that travel screening is beneficial however, quarantining is more effective in detecting  infected travellers. Increasing quarantine duration decreases the proportion of infections missed, but even a 21-day quarantine is predicted to be imperfect if the incubation period distribution is heavy-tailed. Empirical data to better characterize the shape of this distribution would greatly improve predictions and therefore, quarantine decisions must weigh the increasing cost of longer quarantine periods against the diminishing benefit of reduced risk of evasion.
Methods Summary: Researchers  developed a simple model to predict the proportion of infected travelers missed by symptom screening at arrival and post-arrival quarantine with symptom monitoring. This model analyzes the effectiveness of symptom-based screening and quarantine measures in detecting infected travelers, considering variables like incubation period, symptom onset, and epidemiological dynamics. It derives failure rates for screening and quarantine programs under different scenarios, including steady-state and exponentially growing outbreaks, and provides estimates for the Bundibugyo virus incubation period
Citation: Middleton, C., & Larremore, D. (2026). Limitations of cross-border containment strategies for bundibugyo ebolavirus. medRxiv, doi:10.64898/2026.06.04.26354820

Reference: Mooring, et al, (2026), MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep.
Title: Modeled Scenario Projections for the Ebola Disease Outbreak Caused by Bundibugyo Virus, 2026
Key summary: With poor isolation levels of patients with BVD (20%) and no other interventions, the likelihood of an outbreak that exceeds 20,000 cases within 3 months is 65%. If, however a high proportion of patients were to enter isolation (70%), only a one in 20 chance is projected for an outbreak with ?10,000 cases within 3 months.
Foci: Reproduction Number; Size or Duration estimate; Mortality
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration & Clinical Data - Mortality: The transmission model estimated the outbreak likely began between late January and mid-February 2026.  With only 20% of cases isolated, simulations frequently predicted >20,000 cases and >4,000 deaths. Increasing isolation to 70% substantially reduced transmission, with most simulations projecting <10,000 cases and <2,000 deaths. However, if the outbreak was already larger by May 24 (100–200 deaths), even 70% isolation still resulted in a substantial risk of very large outbreaks.

Epidemiological Parameter - Reproduction Number (R0/Rt): SUPPLEMENTARY FIGURE 1 shows estimates of the effective reproductive number before and after  simulated implementation of isolation of persons with Bundibugyo virus disease, 2026. Re declined proportional to the percentage of infected persons successfully isolated.
Methods Summary: CDC used a branching process model to simulate BVD outbreaks where each simulated outbreak was initialized with one infected person, who represented the person first infected from a zoonotic source (a spillover event). This person infected a randomly generated number of additional persons based on assumptions about the basic reproductive number ([R0]. Any infected persons were added to the simulation at times selected according to the distribution of intervals from one infection to the next, causing further infection and this simulation process continued until either 1) none of the infected persons in a generation caused any secondary infections, indicating termination of the outbreak or 2) the simulation reached 5,000 deaths, indicating a very large and exponentially growing outbreak.
Citation: Mooring, E. Q., Koval, W. T., Routledge, I., Holmdahl, I., EspaĂ±a, G., Kahn, R., & Bruce, B. B. (2026). Modeled scenario projections for the ebola disease outbreak caused by bundibugyo virus, 2026. MMWR Morb Mortal Wkly Rep., doi:http://dx.doi.org/10.15585/mmwr.mm7522e1
