Title: Clinical Profile and Genomic Characterization of the 2026 Bundibugyo Virus Index Case in Uganda
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Descriptive, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: This is a case report of the Uganda 2026 BVD index case: a male patient who traveled from the Democratic Republic of the Congo to Uganda and was admitted to a private hospital in Kampala on May 11 and died on May 14. The 2026 BDBV genome formed a distinct lineage approximately equidistant from the 2007-2008 Butalya and 2012 Isiro variants, differing by 216-227 nucleotides (~1.2% sequence divergence).
Foci: Acute infection characteristics; Mortality; Hospitalizations; Phylogenetic analysis/molecular epidemiology
Outcome Summary:
Clinical Data - The patient’s clinical history revealed a complex cross-border pattern of healthcare-seeking behavior: he reportedly sought medical care in the DRC and later at a private clinic in Hoima District, western Uganda, where no blood work was performed before he left, and then self-referred to a private hospital in Kampala. The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit on 14 May with sepsis and multiorgan dysfunction, acute fulminant hepatitis, acute kidney injury, atrial flutter with a rapid ventricular response, and possible disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). He developed sudden cardiorespiratory arrest, and death was declared after unsuccessful  cardiopulmonary resuscitation. 

Phylogenetic analysis - Phylogenetic analysis showed that the 2026 Uganda genome forms a distinct lineage relative to both the 2007–2008 Uganda and 2012 DRC BDBV genomes. Pairwise genome comparisons  identified 219 nucleotide differences between the 2026 genome and the 2007–2008 Uganda genomes, and 216–227 nucleotide differences relative to the 2012 DRC genomes, corresponding 158 to approximately 1.2% sequence divergence across comparable nucleotide sites.
Methods Summary: Clinical case report of index BVD case. Demographic information, including patient sex and travel history from the DRC, was extracted from a standardized national VHF case investigation form and the electronic case admission record. EDTA whole-blood specimen was collected before death and tested via RT-qPCR,  Genome sequencing and phylogenetic analysis.
Citation: Nsawotebba, A., Ssewanyana, I., Ayitewala, A., Wayengera, M., Kanyerezi, S., Kabazzi, J., . . . Atwine, D. (2026). Clinical profile and genomic characterization of the 2026 bundibugyo virus index case in uganda. Nature Medicine, doi:10.1038/s41591-026-04510-7

Reference: NzanzuTJ, et al, (2026), Student's Journal of Health Research Africa
Title: Understanding the epidemiology of Ebola Bundibugyo virus (EBV) in selected populations: a cross-sectional study in Buhendera and Bwamba counties, Bundibugyo district
Key summary: In a cross-sectional study carried out in 6 sites, risk factors, distribution, endemicity and mode of action and transmission of the Ebola-Bundibugyo (EBV) strain after the 2007 outbreak were examined. This study found that EBV was influenced by gender roles (males more exposed through hunting/gathering), limited healthcare access, religious affiliation (Catholics most affected), occupation (farmers near reservoirs at higher risk), low income (increased hunting/gathering), larger family size, cultural practices (sleeping near/washing the dead), climatic conditions (harvest season increasing human-animal contact), and human movement patterns (with Bundibugyo as a central hub).
Foci: Infection risk factors; Mortality; pathogen structural characterization
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION:
Gender: Males were at higher risk due to activities like hunting and gathering wild fruits (58.6% vs. 41.4% females; p < 0.05).

Healthcare Access: Limited access was significant, with 72.2% reporting relatives lacking medical care (p < 0.001).

Religion: Catholics experienced the highest cases (24.5%), followed by SDA (12.9%) and Muslims (17.7%; p < 0.05).

Occupation: Farming near animal reservoirs was critical, as the first outbreak occurred in a maize farmer (p < 0.01).

Income: Low income increased reliance on high-risk activities (p < 0.05).

Family Size: Larger family size showed a significant linear relationship with EBV occurrence (p < 0.001).

Tribe: Tribal differences had no significant impact (p > 0.05).

Climate: Bimodal rainfall and harvesting seasons heightened human-animal interaction (p < 0.05).

Structural characterization: EBV under an electron microscope is an anatomically  helical,  single-stranded,  unsegmented  RNA negative sense virus. Envelope glycoprotein is initially produced as a precursor known as pre-GP, cleaved by furin into two subunits, GP1 and GP2, which remain associated through a disulfide linkage between Cys53 of GP1 and Cys609 of GP2.
Methods Summary: This cross-section observational study in Bundibugyo District, Western Uganda, investigated Ebola Bundibugyo virus (EBV) using interviews with farmers, climatic assessments, and data from hospital and district health records. Snowball sampling identified households affected by EBV, while questionnaires and interview guides collected data on cultural risk factors (e.g., sleeping near/washing the dead, burial rituals). EBV’s mode of action, structure, and biochemistry were analyzed using IgG ELISA on preserved samples at UVRI and CDC literature reviews.
Citation: Nzanzu T.J., Kiwanuka S.N., Tinzara W., Salum S.S., Twesigye C.K., Mugenyi H., & Tugume G. (2026). Understanding the epidemiology of ebola bundibugyo virus (EBV) in selected populations: A cross-sectional study in buhendera and bwamba counties, bundibugyo district. Student's Journal of Health Research Africa, 7(2) doi:10.51168/sjhrafrica.v7i2.2342

Title: A Single-Dose Bundibugyo Virus Vaccine Protects Macaques Within 3 Days
Key summary: Authors evaluated the rapid protection by a single-dose vaccination of vesicular stomatitis virus - Bundibugyo virus (VSV-BDBV) in  cynomolgus macaques (n=5) and showed that vaccination elicited rapid innate and early adaptive immune responses and conferred complete protection from clinical disease against BDBV challenge within 3 days. Vaccinated cynomolgus macaques exhibited minimal clinical signs, limited systemic inflammation, and no infectious virus was isolated from the blood at any time; protection correlated with neutralizing antibodies and Fc effector functionality of the humoral immune response.
Foci: Vaccination immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines)
Outcome Summary:
Vaccine immunity - Protection from infection and other disease outcomes (efficacy/effectiveness): A control group received the VSV-Marburg virus (VSV-MARV) vaccine at 1×107 PFU by the  intramuscular route 7 days prior to BDBV challenge and resulted in 60% lethality. Viral load quantification showed that the control non-human primates (NHP) developed high titer viremia, specifically the NHPs that succumbed to disease, while the VSV-BDBV-vaccinated NHPs displayed no or low viral RNA levels. No infectious virus was recovered at any time point from the blood of all vaccinated NHPs, BDBV RNA was absent in all swab samples obtained from the vaccinated NHPs

Vaccine immunity - Measures of protective immunity (antibodies/titres/cytokines): The demonstration of rapid onset of protection for species-specific VSV-based vaccines against the human-pathogenic filoviruses indicates that this may be an intrinsic property of the VSV platform dominated early on by the robust innate immune response. The protective efficacy of VSV-based filovirus vaccines altogether, however, is species-specific, driven primarily by the antibody responses directed against the viral GP antigens. Rapid protection appears to be associated with the induction of early antiviral innate immune responses coupled with the accelerated development of functional GP-specific antibodies. Consistent with a benign clinical disease course, vaccinated NHPs exhibited minimal cytokine dysregulation following challenge. Pro-inflammatory cytokines characteristic of severe EBOD, including IL-6, TNF-?, IFN-?, IFN-?, and IFN-?, were markedly reduced compared to the control NHPs.
Methods Summary: Fifteen male cynomolgus macaques (Macaca fascicularis) 6-11 years of age and 5.5–7.9 kg in weight were used for this study and randomly assigned to vaccination groups. Group sizes of n = 5 were determined to be required by power analysis using log-rank test in graph pad/prism under the assumption that 40% of the control animals may survive the infection. To determine a shortened time from vaccination to challenge resulting in uniform protection from disease caused by BDBV infection, cynomolgus macaques (n=5 per group) received a single intramuscular (IM) dose of VSV–BDBV (1×107 PFU) either 7 or 3 days prior to IM challenge with 10,000 median tissue culture infectious dose (TCID50) of BDBV. A control group received the VSV-Marburg virus (VSV-MARV) vaccine at 1×107 PFU by the IM route 7 days prior to BDBV challenge. The total white blood cell, neutrophil, lymphocyte, and platelet counts were determined from EDTA blood with the IDEXX ProCyte DX analyzer (IDEXX Laboratories, Westbrook, ME). Serum biochemistry including aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), glucose, blood urea nitrogen, creatinine, and total bilirubin was analyzed on a Vetscan 2 using Preventive care profile disks (Abaxis, Union City, CA).
Citation: O'Donnell, K., Haase, J., Henderson, C., Gathright, R., Fletcher, P., Rhoderick, J., . . . Marzi, A. (2026). A single-dose bundibugyo virus vaccine protects macaques within 3 days bioRxiv. doi:10.64898/2026.06.14.732188

Reference: Okingo, et al, (2026), Journal of travel medicine
Title: Estimating the introduction risk and sustained transmission of the Ebola Bundibugyo outbreak 2026 in the Democratic Republic of Congo
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Modeling, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: The authors estimated introduction risk and sustained transmission potential of the Bundibugyo virus outbreak in the Ituri province, Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC). They found that introduction risk was highest in the health zones close to the reported  foci, specifically in Ituri, Nord-Kivu, and Haut-Uele. High sustained-transmission potential was distributed across DRC, including Kinshasa, Kasaď-Oriental, Kongo-Central, Tshopo, Nord-Kivu, Haut-Katanga, Sud-Kivu, Kasaď, and Kasaď-Central, suggesting preparedness in health zones with high sustained-transmission potential but lower introduction risk.
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - Estimates of outbreak size or duration: 

The authors estimated the potential for an introduced case to generate sustained transmission in the DRC and found that introduction risk was highest in health zones (HZs) close to the reported foci and high sustained-transmission potential was distributed across DRC,  including Kinshasa, KasaďOriental, Kongo-Central, Tshopo, Nord-Kivu, Haut-Katanga, Sud-Kivu, Kasaď, and Kasaď-Central.  Crossing the two rankings identified eight HZs that combined high introduction risk and high sustained-transmission potential: Binza Ozone and Binza Meteo in Kinshasa; Karisimbi in Nord-Kivu; Kadutu and Ibanda in Sud-Kivu; and Makiso-Kisangani, Kabondo, and Mangobo in Tshopo.
Methods Summary: The authors used multitype branching processes to estimate  for each DRC health zone (HZ), the potential for an introduced case to generate sustained transmission. They used  available population and mobility data to parameterize spatial transmission, testing a broad range of assumptions on disease transmissibility and on how local and between-community contact patterns can be inferred from these data. They then ranked HZs by sustained-transmission potential and compared them with introduction-risk ranks estimated from mobility data.

Title: The Impact of Diagnostic Delays and Timeliness of Response on Ebola Disease outbreak-level case-fatality Ratios in Uganda (2000–2023): a Rapid Systematic Review and meta-analysis
Key summary: This rapid review and meta-analysis aimed to quantify the effect of diagnostic and response delays on outbreak-level mortality for Ebola virus disease (EBOD) in Uganda (2000-2022).  By species, Sudan ebolavirus outbreaks (n?=?5) had a CFR of 44.6% (95% CI: 33.7%–55.6%), Bundibugyo ebolavirus (n?=?1) 24.8% (95% CI: 18.2%–32.1%), and Zaire ebolavirus (n?=?1) 100% (95% CI: 61.2%–100.0%).
Foci: Other Epi; Mortality; Mortality risk factors
Outcome Summary:
EPIDEMIOLOIGCAL PARAMETER OTHER:
In the subsequent Bundibugyo 2007 epidemic, Ministry of Health operations including rapid case notification and mobilization of contact?tracing teams achieved a mean interval of three days from index case report to completion of contact line?listing.


MORTALITY:
CFR for Bundibugyo ebolavirus (n?=?1) 24.8% (95% CI: 18.2%–32.1%) which was lower than Zaire ebolavirus (100% CFR) and Sudan  (44.6% CFR).

RISK FACTORS- MORTALITY:
Bundibugyo ebolavirus patients and found that haemorrhagic manifestations were significantly more common in those who died (Fisher’s exact p = 0.05) and that each additional clinically observed symptom increased the odds of death by ~ 31% (OR 1.31; 95% CI 1.04–1.82) .
Methods Summary:
We searched PubMed, Embase, Scopus, Web of Science, WHO Global Index Medicus, and grey literature through 30 April 2025. Eligible reports described laboratory-confirmed human EBOD in Uganda (2000–2022) and reported case counts, deaths, or quantitative timeliness metrics. Outbreak-level CFRs were meta-analyzed using random-effects models with Freeman–Tukey transformation (metafor package in R). Mixed-effects meta-regression assessed the association between continuous delay metrics and transformed CFR.

 We synthesized data from 15 studies covering seven laboratory-confirmed Ebola outbreaks in Uganda (2000–2022) (Table 2), comprising four Sudan ebolavirus waves (Gulu 2000; Luwero 2011; Kibaale 2012; Luwero hospital 2012), one Bundibugyo ebolavirus wave (Bundibugyo 2007), and two Zaire ebolavirus importations (Kasese 2019 and 2020).

Title: Rapid risk assessment: Ebola disease caused by Bundibugyo virus in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Uganda
Key summary:
The overall risk level to the Canadian population of acquiring Bundibugyo virus from the ongoing outbreak in the DRC and Uganda is currently low (moderate uncertainty). 

The likelihood of importation of Bundibugyo virus into Canada in the next two weeks is low (moderate uncertainty), given the low travel volume between currently affected regions and Canada, and existing travel advisories that recommend against all travel to the outbreak-affected areas in the DRC due to ongoing safety and security concerns. The impact on an affected individual is estimated to be severe (low uncertainty), given the serious clinical manifestations of BVD, its high case fatality rate and lack of approved vaccines or antivirals.

If importation into Canada were to occur, the impact of BVD on the general population in Canada would be minor (low uncertainty), due to the limited number of secondary cases anticipated among close contacts, strong diagnostic and health system capacity, and existing protocols for outbreak response, case and contact management and infection prevention and control.

Title: Genomic Epidemiology and Molecular Characteristics of Bundibugyo Virus
Key summary: This rapid review highlights the genomic and molecular characteristics of BDBV, focusing on mutations identified in recent and past outbreaks; comparative genomic analyses based on current available genomic sequences indicate that the 2026 outbreak likely originated from a novel zoonotic spillover event rather than direct continuation of the 2007 Uganda or 2012 DRC outbreaks. Based on genomic analysis, shared nonsynonymous substitutions, concentrated in antigenically exposed regions of the glycoprotein (GP) gene, were observed in the 2012 and 2026 outbreak genomes.
Outcome Summary: MUTATIONS/PHYLOGENETIC ANALYSIS:  Genomic analyses suggest this outbreak stems from a novel zoonotic spillover, distinct from the 2007 and 2012 outbreaks, with shared and unique mutations identified in the glycoprotein (GP) gene and other viral regions. However, the functional impact of these mutations on viral transmissibility, pathogenicity, or fitness remains experimentally unconfirmed.
Methods Summary: Researchers reviewed published and preprint literature for BDBV mutations potentially associated with outbreak dynamics and viral evolution. Keywords were searched in PubMed and sequence data from the 2026 outbreak and previously reported outbreak-associated sequences  were retrieved from the  SeqSet and analyzed for genomic relatedness.
Citation: Public Health Ontario. (2026b). Genomic epidemiology and molecular characteristics of bundibugyo virus. Title: Estimating the effectiveness of syndromic screening at airports for Bundibugyo ebolavirus disease
Key summary: For a 12-hour international flight from DRC or Uganda at 86% screening sensitivity, the authors estimated 65% of infected travellers would arrive undetected (95% CrI: 38–76%). The main driver of this outcome is the relative duration of the the incubation period (approximately 7.7 days) and the onset-to-severe-disease interval (approximately 4 days): most infected travellers board before symptom onset and are undetectable by any syndromic screen, whilst those who are symptomatic progress rapidly to illness severe enough to preclude travel.
Outcome Summary: Public Health Measures - Border measures: At 86% sensitivity on a 12-hour international flight, combined syndromic exit and entry screening will fail to detect approximately 65% of infected travellers (95% CrI: 38–76%). The relatively short onset-to-severe-disease interval (mean approximately four days) means that symptomatic travellers progress to detectable illness quickly, yet the incubation period (approximately 7.7 days) means that most infected travellers will board before symptom onset. Exit syndromic screening sensitivity has a larger marginal effect on the undetected fraction than entry screening sensitivity, because it acts on a larger at-risk window: any traveller already symptomatic at departure can be caught at exit, whereas entry screening only adds value for those who become symptomatic during the flight or who were missed due to imperfect screening at exit. When exit screening is absent entirely (sensitivity 0%) but entry screening is maintained at 86%, 67% of travellers remain undetected, compared with 73% when entry screening is absent and exit is maintained at 86%. This asymmetry reflects the longer opportunity for symptom detection that the pre-flight period provides relative to the flight itself.
Methods Summary: Authors used a stochastic individual-based simulation model to evaluate the probability that a Bundibugyo ebolavirus disease (BVD) infected traveller would be detected at (i) exit screening before departure, (ii) as severely ill during the flight, (iii) at entry screening on arrival, or (iv) remain undetected throughout, using natural-history parameters from a Bayesian re-analysis of the 2012 Isiro outbreak.  Natural-history parameters (incubation period, onset-to-severe-disease delay) are derived from the 2007 Uganda and 2012 DRC BVD outbreaks. The epidemic doubling time range used in the growth-phase sensitivity analysis is estimated from the current outbreak.
Citation: Quilty, B. (2026). Estimating the effectiveness of syndromic screening at airports for bundibugyo ebolavirus disease. medRxiv, doi:10.64898/2026.06.11.26355442

Title: Recommendations: Rapid Synthesis of Social and Behavioural Science learnings on Ebola for the Bundibugyo Virus Outbreak (2026) Ituri, DRC
Publication Details: Other evidence synthesis, Other grey literature report
Key summary: This briefing outlines key considerations and recommendations drawn from a rapid synthesis of lessons learned from prior social and behavioural science (SBS) research highlighting that Ebola response in Ituri must prioritize trust-building by addressing historical grievances and avoiding coercive tactics, with tailored infection prevention, contact tracing, and border management that respect local social networks and languages. Additionally, emphasis is placed on effective community engagement, which requires partnering with trusted leaders, vulnerable groups, and high-risk occupations (e.g., boda boda drivers) while adapting safe burials and risk communication to cultural contexts and actionable, multilingual guidance.
Foci: KAB and uptake; Quarantine - PHM; Border measure - PHM; Workflow guidance - IPC; Indirect -  lifestyle
Outcome Summary:
Public Health Measures: Border communities between DRC and neighboring countries are deeply interconnected, with daily cross-border movement for essential activities like farming, water collection, market access, and healthcare which is driven by perceived better health services in Uganda. Past messaging restricting border crossings pushed people toward riskier, undetectable routes (panya routes), while boda boda taxi drivers and informal trade networks (including state-involved smuggling) continue to facilitate movement, even after Uganda’s shutdown of lake border crossings, which persist informally via unregulated points. Community networks can improve early disease detection and access to care; past evidence shows cases were referred to an integrated treatment centre with EVD testing and isolation. 

Infection Prevention and Control (IPC) - General workflow guidance: IPC guidance needs to be  in community health facilities, where health worker fatalities have occurred, and extended to caregivers and high-risk groups like boda boda drivers and market vendors. In eastern DRC, heightened fear among these public-facing occupations requires actionable, tailored IPC measures that prioritize safety without stigmatization, addressing their unique risks while maintaining community trust.

Indirect impacts - Social (lifestyle/behavior changes): Past SBS research in eastern DRC on Ebola outbreaks has shown an ‘atmosphere of mistrust’ in interactions between responders and communities, rooted in historical grievances like colonial violence, perceived humanitarian exploitation, and political manipulation (e.g., "Ebola as a business" during the 2018–20 outbreak). This mistrust is fueled by inconsistent resource allocation, contested authority, and unmet humanitarian needs during non-epidemic periods, making fear-based or context-blind responses likely to fail. However, SBS research demonstrates that localized, trust-building actions such as family-inclusive treatment centers, respectful burials, and media engagement with trusted community figures can humanize the response and foster cooperation
Methods Summary: The authors conducted a rapid synthesis including lessons learned from prior Ebola social and behavioural science (SBS) research and highlights critical insights for response efforts. It highlights critical insights for locally adapted and contextually informed response efforts that take account of the broader humanitarian crisis.
Citation: Schweitzer, K. (2026). A rare ebola virus is spreading in the DRC-here's what to know. JAMA, doi:10.1001/jama.2026.10042

Title: Bundibugyo virus disease evidence map: a Rapid Research Needs Appraisal
Key summary: This rapid research needs appraisal synthesises existing published evidence across key domains of BDBV, including clinical presentation, complications, and long-term sequelae, identified 25 studies, predominantly observational (96%), conducted in Uganda (n=15), DRC (n=8), and Guinea (n=2). Fever, fatigue, headache, and gastrointestinal symptoms were most commonly reported; haemorrhagic manifestations ranged from 29–52%, with a CFR of 32.2% (CI 25.8–40.2).
Foci: Infection risk factors; Attack Rate or SAR; Incubation / Latent period; Acute infection characteristics; Long term infection characteristics; Mortality; Severity risk factors; Infection immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); KAB and uptake; Thearapeutics; DTA; Indirect - violence
Outcome Summary: This rapid research needs appraisal synthesises existing published evidence across key domains, including clinical presentation, complications, and long-term sequelae, identified 25 studies, predominantly observational (96%), conducted in Uganda (n=15), DRC (n=8), and Guinea (n=2).  This appraisal identified a sparse evidence-base with profound gaps in clinical characterisation, therapeutics, vaccines, and social and behavioural research. Existing countermeasures target EBOV and cannot be assumed effective against BDBV. The ongoing outbreak in DRC and Uganda makes these gaps not a future priority but an immediate crisis.
Methods Summary: Researchers implemented a targeted RRNA focused on Bundibugyo virus to identify studies including humans or human samples, focused on studying at least one of the research domain topics: clinical characteristics, immune response, seroprevalence, transmission (human-to-human), risk factors for disease, severe disease/death, diagnostics, vaccines and therapeutic prophylaxis, therapeutics, supportive care, and associated social and behavioural factors. Following PRISMA-ScR guidelines, protocol registration: https://osf.io/twfnr/13The following databases were searched: Ovid Embase, Ovid MEDLINE and Scopus from 1 January 1999 to 06 February 2026.

Title: The risk of global Ebola virus spread is low: epidemiology of Ebola disease cases outside Africa, 1976 to May 2026
Publication Details: Primary - article, Observational, Other grey literature report
Key summary: This study assessed global Ebola transmission by evaluating 28 confirmed Ebola cases outside of Africa (25 primary imported cases and three secondary cases in the US or Europe) of which 27 occurred in 2014-2016 and one case in May 2026. The crude overall risk of exportation was 0.81 cases per 1000 Ebola cases outside of Africa including medically evacuated, latent and secondary cases.  The four latent cases from 300,000 were asymptomatic at screening at exit and entry, suggesting clear guidance on self-monitoring of symptoms for rapid isolation.
Foci: Infection risk factors; Size or Duration estimate; Mortality; Hospitalizations; Other clinical data; Border measure - PHM
Population: Human - General population, Human - Healthcare workers
Outcome Summary:
PHM- BORDER MEASURES:
 The four latent cases identified occurred among 300,000 travellers screened by the US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)-supported exit screening programmes in Guinea, Liberia and Sierra Leone, and all were asymptomatic (and hence undetectable) at the point of both exit screening and entry screening. Screening protocols included clear guidance on procedures for self-monitoring of symptoms [9], which ultimately led to rapid confirmation and isolation among the three latent cases in medical workers.

CLINICAL DATA: The study identified four latent Ebola cases exported during the 2014–16 epidemic, all initially asymptomatic at screening but later presenting with acute symptoms (fever, respiratory distress, weakness) that required hospitalization. These cases highlight the challenge of early detection, as three were healthcare workers who self-isolated upon symptom onset, while one traveler was misdiagnosed initially. In Europe, 17 confirmed Ebola cases were found since 1976 of whom, 16 occurred during the 2014-2016 Ebola disease epidemic and one during the 2026 Bundibugyo virus epidemic. In North America, 11 confirmed Ebola cases were identified since 1976 and all 11 cases occurred during the 2014-16 Ebola epidemic. Overall, of the 28 confirmed Ebola disease cases, five died. All three secondary cases were due to nosocomial infection of healthcare workers treating a known Ebola disease patient.

Epidemiological Parameter - RISK FACTORS FOR INFECTION: Of the 28 confirmed cases identified, three secondary cases were due to nosocomial infection of healthcare workers treating a known Ebola disease patient. 

The vast majority of Ebola cases outside Africa (27 of 28) were either primary cases in individuals with occupational/exposure-related roles (e.g., healthcare workers, responders) or secondary cases in healthcare workers treating imported patients. Only one case occurred in a traveler without such exposure, which is concerning as it led to further transmission. However, the overall risk of exportation remains low and can be effectively reduced through infection control measures, travel screening, and monitoring of response workers, as aligned with WHO guidelines.

Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES FOR OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION:
 The authors calculated a crude risk estimate of 0.81 cases outside Africa, including medically evacuated, latent and secondary cases, per 1,000 Ebola disease cases in a source outbreak.  Focusing on latent cases and their secondary cases only, there have been 0.17 cases outside Africa per 1,000 Ebola disease cases in the source outbreak. 

For cases exported from the 2014–16 epidemic in Western Africa, we carried out a time-varying risk analysis using Poisson regression of the number of exported cases for a given epidemiological week and country. Over the course of the epidemic, the case exportation risk per reported source outbreak case decreased by 4.6% per week (95% CI: 1.7–7.4%; p?=?0.0097). More generally, the case exportation risk was substantially lower after the epidemic peak (0.46; 95% CI: 0.17–0.73 exported cases per 1,000 source cases from 27 October 2014 onwards) than it was before the peak (1.7; 95% CI: 0.95–2.7 exported cases per 1,000 source cases). This represents a 73% reduction in exportation risk per outbreak case between these two periods.
Methods Summary: Researchers used manual and AI-assisted internet searches to identify all laboratory-confirmed Ebola cases outside Africa, reviewing scientific articles, public health bulletins, and news reports to compile data from 27 studies, 9 bulletins, 82 news articles, and 9 other sources. The study included only confirmed cases of Bundibugyo, Ebola, and Sudan virus infections, excluding suspected cases, lab-acquired injuries, and Reston virus infections, to focus on epidemiologically relevant human transmissions.
Citation: van Zandvoort, K., Procter, S. R., Azam, J. M., Sherratt, K., & Davies, N. G. (2026). The risk of global ebola virus spread is low: Epidemiology of ebola disease cases outside africa, 1976 to may 2026. Euro Surveillance: Bulletin Europeen Sur Les Maladies Transmissibles = European Communicable Disease Bulletin, 31(24) doi:10.2807/1560-7917.ES.2026.31.24.2600508

Title: Projected epidemiologic and economic impact of the 7-1-7 outbreak response framework in Uganda: a stochastic modelling study of Bundibugyo Ebola virus
Key summary: This study uses a counterfactual scenario modelling approach to answer: "what would be the projected epidemiologic and economic impact if Uganda implements a rapid 7-1-7 (detection ?7 days, notification ?1 day, response ?7 days) compared with a delayed response, under varying importation pressures?" by using a rapid 3-1-5 response (detection 3 days, notification 1 day, response 5 days) against a delayed counterfactual (detection 11 days, notification 10 days,response 12 days),. They found that a rapid 3-1-5 response (detection 3 days, notification 1 day, response 5 days) reduces median cumulative cases by 6066% and deaths by 62–63% compared to a delayed counterfactual (11-10-12 days), across import probabilities from 10% to the observed 56%; the rapid response is strictly dominant (cost-saving and life-saving) for all import levels, with total costs of USD 29.1–29.9 million versus USD 37.4–38.1 million for the delayed scenario; the case fatality rate, import probability, and basic reproduction number are the dominant drivers of outcome uncertainty.
Foci: Size or Duration estimate; Mortality; Economic cost of disease
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION:

Rapid response: Median cumulative cases ranged from 26-31 with a narrow interquartile range (21-39).  Under the rapid 3-1-5 response, cumulative infections rise slowly and plateau below 40 cases by day 90, with overlapping uncertainty bands across import levels. 

Delayed response: the delayed scenario produced substantially larger outbreaks: median cases ranged from 76 (10% import) to 80 (30% import) and 78 (56% import), with upper 95% uncertainty limits exceeding 120 cases.  

CLINICAL DATA: MORTALITY pg15
Rapid response:   Median deaths were 3 across all import levels, and health worker infections ranged from 6 to 8. 

Delayed response: Median deaths were 8 for all delayed scenarios, health worker infections were 27–31, 

ECONOMIC COSTS

Rapid response:   Total costs were approximately USD 29.1–29.9 million, and DALYs were 101 for all import levels. 

Delayed scenario:  Total costs were USD 37.4–38.1 million. 

DALYs in the delayed scenario were 270–285, approximately 2.7 times higher than in the rapid response. The rapid 3-1-5 scenario therefore averted 52–54 deaths per 100,000 population compared to the delayed response, with cost savings of USD 7.5–8.6 million per import level. The incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) was negative for all comparisons, confirming that the rapid response is strictly dominant (less costly and more effective)
Methods Summary:
This study uses a stochastic SEIR model to evaluate the *7-1-7  ( (detection ?7 days, notification ?1 day, response ?7 days) outbreak response framework in Uganda with the Bundibugyo Ebola strain  Using a stochastic SEIR model parameterised for Uganda with the Bundibugyo Ebola strain and three daily importation probabilities (10%, 30%, and the observed 56%), we compared a rapid 3-1-5 response (detection 3 days, notification 1 day, response 5 days) against a delayed counterfactual (detection 11 days, notification 10 days, response 12 days).  The Sobol global sensitivity analysis was used to quantify the contribution of 16 parameters to the uncertainty in total deaths. Variance-based Sobol sensitivity analysis identified the case fatality rate, import probability, and basic reproduction number as the most influential parameters, with detection and response delays contributing through interactions.


All parameters (Table 1) were obtained from the Uganda Ministry of Health Situation Report No. 005 (26 May 2026) (MOH Uganda, 2026) and the updated Situation Report of 8 June 2026 (MOH, 8 June 2026), the WHO risk assessment (WHO PHEIC, 2026), a meta-analysis of Bundibugyo strain 0 (Muzembo et al., 2024), and literature on Ebola transmission dynamics (Althaus, 2014; Velasquez et al., 2015). The 0 was set to 2.76 – the upper bound of the 95% confidence interval (1.25–2.76) reported for the Bundibugyo strain – to reflect the most transmissible scenario and provide a conservative test of the rapid response. 


Costs were estimated from the health system perspective in 2026 USD. The Uganda Ministry of Health’s 4W matrix (Who is doing What, Where, and When) provided a detailed 90-day fixed response budget of USD 24,707,459, disaggregated into 11 operational pillars. Because most of these costs are fixed regardless of outbreak size (e.g., maintaining the Emergency Operations Centre, laboratories, and coordination structures), we derived a cost per case from the median case count under the rapid 7-1-7 scenario with the lowest importation risk (10%). The median number of cases in that scenario was 3, giving a cost per case of USD 24,707,459 / 3 ? USD 8,235,820. For other scenarios, total cost = cost per case × median cases. Disability-adjusted life years (DALYs) were calculated as DALY = YLL + YLD. Years of life lost (YLL) = deaths × (life expectancy at age of death ? age at death), using average age at death = 35 years and life expectancy at birth for Uganda = 68 years (WHO 2023).   Years lived with disability (YLD) = cases × disability weight × duration, with disability weight for acute EVD = 0.133 and duration of acute disability = 180 days (0.493 years) (GBD 2021 Disability Weights Collaborators, 2024). The incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) compared the delayed scenario (baseline) to the rapid 7-1-7 scenario (intervention) for each import level.
Citation: Walekhwa, A., Silal, S. P., Mbaka, P., Bbuye, M., Nakiire, L., Lamorde, M., . . . Atwine, D. (2026). Projected epidemiologic and economic impact of the 7-1-7 outbreak response framework in uganda: A stochastic modelling study of bundibugyo ebola virus Research Square. doi:10.21203/rs.3.rs-10019597/v1

Title: Network-based modelling of Bundibugyo Ebola virus disease importation and spread in Uganda using Displacement Tracking Matrix flow data and non-pharmaceutical intervention compliance scenarios
Key summary: This study assesses whether costly nationwide non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs) are needed when the 7-1-7 agile response (detection within 3 days, notification within 1 day, response within 5 days) already includes a strong behavioural component. Mobility data from the Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) showed Kisoro had the highest import (in-strength 3,823) and export (out-strength 1,350) risk, while Kampala had substantial import risk (in-strength 1,290) but lower export risk (out-strength 150); under baseline mobility, the model projected a median of 69 to 70 cumulative cases (95% CrI: 57 to 98) and 3 deaths over 90 days, with NPIs (community contact reduction, healthcare protection, movement restriction) at 20%, 40%, and 60% compliance showing no statistically significant case reduction.
Outcome Summary:
Epidemiological Parameter - ESTIMATES OF OUTBREAK SIZE AND DURATION: 

Under baseline mobility, the model projected a median of 69–70 cumulative cases (95% CrI: 57–98) , 11 367 HCW infections, over 90 days. Non-pharmaceutical interventions (community contact reduction, healthcare protection, movement restriction) at 20%, 40%, and 60% compliance produced no statistically significant reduction in cases. Superspreading events occurred in 34.6–40.6% of simulations. Kampala bore the highest predicted burden (median 22 cases, 100% outbreak probability more than 10 cases), followed by Wakiso (11 cases, 64.9%). Border districts had lower burdens (Bundibugyo 5, Kasese 3, Kisoro 2).

Sobol sensitivity analysis (500 samples, 200 bootstraps) identified the infectious period (first-order index 0.838), case fatality rate (0.738), and basic reproduction number (0.664) as the most influential parameters; mobility-related parameters had lower total-order indices.

MORTALITY:
Under baseline mobility, the model projected 3 deaths and 1 HCW deaths, over 90 days.
Methods Summary:
The authors constructed a data-driven directed weighted mobility network using the International Organization for Migration Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) flows collected from 15–24 May 2026 (11,245 observed movements) and the 2024 Uganda census (45.9 million people). A stochastic metapopulation SEIR model, incorporating pre-symptomatic transmission and the movement of both exposed and infectious individuals, was simulated over 90 days across 135 Ugandan districts and two DRC provinces. Authors simulated three compliance levels (20%, 40%, 60%) for NPIs (risk communication, standard operating procedures, avoidance of handshaking, mass gathering bans) and assessed their impact on total cases, deaths, and healthcare worker (HCW) infections. A global sensitivity analysis using the Sobol method was used to identify which parameters most influence the total case count, thereby informing decision-makers on where to focus data collection and intervention efforts.

The median total cases ranged from 69 to 70 across compliance scenarios, with 3 deaths, 11 healthcare worker (HCW) infections, and 1 HCW death. The 95% credible intervals overlapped substantially (58–98 cases for 20% compliance, 57–95 for 60% compliance), indicating no statistically significant difference between compliance levels. The extinction probability was 0% across all scenarios, reflecting that the outbreak was already established at the start of the simulation (16 observed cases as of June 5, 2026). The probability of a large outbreak (?50 cases) was 100% in all scenarios, consistent with the projected median of approximately 70 cases. Superspreading events (?5 secondary infections from a single source in one day) occurred in 40.6% of simulations at 20% compliance, decreasing to 34.8% at 40% compliance and 34.6% at 60% compliance. The mean number of superspreading events per simulation ranged from 0.396 to 0.472, with a maximum of 3 in any single simulation.
Citation: Walekhwa, A., Mbaka, P., Nannyonga, B., Silal, S. P., Bbuye, M., Nakiire, L., . . . Atwine, D. (2026). Network-based modelling of bundibugyo ebola virus disease importation and spread in uganda using displacement tracking matrix flow data and non-pharmaceutical intervention compliance scenarios medRxiv. doi:10.64898/2026.06.22.26356210

Title: Development of Peptide Entry Inhibitors Targeting the Endosomal Receptor NPC1 Binding Site of Orthoebolavirus
Key summary: This study designed peptide inhibitors effective against BDBV based on the Niemann–Pick C1 (NPC1) receptor to target the NPC1 binding site to block viral entry. The cyclized peptide erp1c demonstrated strong in vitro inhibition of BDBV infection (IC?? = 13.73 µM) and partial in vivo protection in mice (25% survival), while its linear counterpart erp1 was less effective (IC?? = 17.89 µM).
Foci: Infection immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Infection immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); pathogen  Protein-inhibitor/binding interactions
Outcome Summary:
Protein-inhibitor/binding interactions:  

Both peptides erp1 and erp1c inhibited BDBV infection with IC50 values of 17.89 µM and 13.73 µM, respectively. Based on the side-by-side assay, peptide erp1c exhibited strong activity only against EBOV and BDBV with IC50 values of 11.6 µM and 11.3 µM, respectively, but no activity was observed against  Amphotropic murine leukemia virus,  suggesting that the inhibition from the peptide erp1c is specific to Orthoebolavirus or filoviruses and not others, because this peptide inhibitor was designed by specifically targeting the NPC1 binding site. The cyclized peptide erp1c (200 µg, twice daily for 7 days) provided partial protection in mice, with 2/8 surviving (25% survival) compared to 0/8 in untreated controls, indicating potential antiviral efficacy against MA EBOV.

After mutating two phenylalanines (FF) into two alanines (AA) in both forms of peptides erp1 (linear) and erp1c (cyclized) for functional analysis, the data displayed that in both linear and cyclized peptides show the mutated FF/AA peptides decreased the inhibition in a dose-dependent manner, compared with the counterpart wildtype peptide. The linear mutant peptide erp1aa increased the IC50 values from 25 µM to 109 µM with an estimated IC50 fold-change of 4.3; the cyclized mutant peptide erp1caa increased the IC50 values from 12.6 µM to 38.5 µM with an estimated IC50 fold-change of 3.0. These observed shifts in the inhibition curves strongly indicated that these two hydrophobic residues contribute significantly to the binding to the hydrophobic receptor binding sites. 

Molecular docking of cyclic peptides erp1c (wildtype) and erp1caa (FF/AA mutant) showed F7/F8 docked into NPC1’s receptor-binding site, mimicking loop 2. Surprisingly, the mutant shifted V9/Y10 to the binding pocket instead of A7/A8 but had lower affinity, confirming F7/F8’s critical role in viral entry inhibition.
Methods Summary: Peptides were synthesized (?95% purity) and viral glycoproteins (EBOV, BDBV) cloned into pCDNA3.1+ for pseudovirus production in 293T cells via PEI transfection. Inhibition assays included TZM-bl cell-based Luciferase tests (IC?? calculation) and HeLa cell immunostaining (infection efficiency via CellProfiler). In vivo studies comprised pharmacokinetic analysis (IV injection, LC-MS/MS) and efficacy testing (subcutaneous peptide treatment post-MA EBOV challenge), with survival, histopathology, and body weight monitored for 21 days under BSL-4 containment.
Citation: Wang, L. L., Alfson, K., Patten, J. J., Mattix, M. E., Goez-Gazi, Y., Avedissian, S. N., . . . Xiang, S. (2026). Development of peptide entry inhibitors targeting the endosomal receptor NPC1 binding site of orthoebolavirus. Pathogens, 15(6) doi:10.3390/pathogens15060640

Title: A highly potent nanobody-based bispecific therapeutic provides broad-spectrum protection against ebolavirus
Key summary: This study identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that neutralize EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV in vitro and protect female rodents against these pathogens. This positions their bispecific antibody as a strong broad-spectrum anti-ebolavirus candidate.
Outcome Summary:
THERAPEUTICS: Identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that protect female rodents against EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV. A bispecific antibody (BA2-1A10) via GS linker-mediated IgG-Fc fusion provided highly potent protection against all three viruses in female rodents model and positions it as a strong broad-spectrum anti-ebolavirus candidate.
PROETIN-INHIBITOR/BINDING INTERACTIONS: Identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that neutralize EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV in vitro. 1A10 and BA2 bind conserved but non-overlapping epitopes near the GP1 base and GP2’s internal fusion loop (IFL), and biochemical analyses revealed their distinct neutralization mechanisms.
Methods Summary: Researchers identified two camelid-derived nanobodies (1A10 and BA2) that neutralize Ebola virus, Sudan virus, and Bundibugyo virus in vitro and provide protection in rodent models, then used cryo-EM and biochemical analyses to determine how each antibody binds distinct conserved epitopes on the viral glycoprotein. Based on these structural insights, they engineered a bispecific antibody (BA2-1A10) using IgG-Fc fusion, and tested its protective efficacy in female rodents as a broad-spectrum therapeutic candidate.
Citation: Wang M., Zhang X., Li W., Yao Y., Li E., Zhang B., . . . Chiu S. (2026). A highly potent nanobody-based bispecific therapeutic provides broad-spectrum protection against ebolavirus. Nature Communications, 17(1), 4040. doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70464-6

Key summary: WHO recommends that Ervebo® should not be used programmatically in response to BDBV outbreaks outside controlled research settings. The document emphasizes that outbreak control should continue to rely on established public health measures, including surveillance, contact tracing, clinical management, infection prevention and control, and community engagement, while calling for accelerated development and evaluation of BDBV-specific and broadly protective filovirus vaccines.

Title: WHO guidelines for the clinical management of filovirus disease: executive summary
Key summary: The guidelines address key clinical interventions to improve outcomes for patients with suspected or confirmed filovirus disease. Recommendations cover systematic clinical and laboratory monitoring, oral and intravenous fluid management, assessment and treatment of shock, use of vasopressors, central venous access, antimicrobial therapy, management of haemorrhage and postpartum haemorrhage, and structured follow-up for survivors. Emphasis is placed on high-quality supportive care, individualized clinical decision-making and integration with infection prevention and control measures.

Reference: Wight, et al, (2026), Emerging infectious diseases
Title: Antibodies Cross-Reactive with Bundibugyo Virus in Ferrets Vaccinated with Ebola Virus Vaccine
Publication Details: Primary - short communication, Experimental, Peer-reviewed publication
Key summary: To establish whether cross-reactive immune responses against  Bundibugyo virus (BDBV) can be elicited by heterologous recombinant vesicular stomatitis virus (rVSV)–based vaccine, the authors sought to evaluate the levels of BDBV-specific IgG in the context of a ferret model. They found that the rVSV-EBOV vaccine elicits a moderate cross-reactive immune response against BDBV, while rVSV-SUDV does not,  suggesting rVSV-EBOV could potentially offer some protection against BDBV, though further research is needed to confirm.
Outcome Summary:
Vaccine immunity - Measures of protective immunity (antibodies/titres/cytokines): 

Authors collected serum samples from ferrets 27 days after vaccination with rVSV-EBOV or rVSV-SUDV and quantified EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV glycoprotein-specific IgG levels by using ELISA. Both rVSV-EBOV and rVSV-SUDV elicited strong humoral responses to their homologous antigens; serum from all animals exhibited maximum absorbance at dilutions of 1:400.  At 16,582, the geometric mean endpoint titer for BDBV glycoprotein-specific IgG was significantly lower than that for EBOV glycoprotein (Wilcoxon statistical test = 3; p = 1.6 × 10–4), despite limited overlap in the range of titers, which extended from 3,200 to 30,270. We detected no EBOV- or BDBV-specific IgG in the control animals. 

The animals survived subsequent challenge with a uniformly lethal dose of the corresponding virus. Serum samples from ferrets vaccinated with rVSV-EBOV exhibited moderate levels of absorbance against BDBV glycoprotein, in contrast with serum samples from animals vaccinated with rVSV-SUDV, which exhibited very low levels of absorbance.  rVSV-EBOV vaccination elicits BDBV-specific antibodies, extending observations that have already been made in humans.
Methods Summary: Animal studies were conducted at the Canadian Science Centre for Human and Animal Health (CSCHAH) under protocols approved by the Institutional Animal Care Committee, adhering to Canadian Council on Animal Care guidelines. Ferrets were vaccinated intramuscularly with rVSV-EBOV or rVSV-SUDV (2 × 10? PFU/dose) or saline (controls), with serum collected pre-vaccination and on day 27 post-vaccination. IgG levels specific to EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV glycoproteins were measured using indirect ELISA, and statistical analysis (Wilcoxon test) compared reciprocal endpoint titers for EBOV and BDBV glycoproteins.
Citation: Wight, J., Schulz, H., & Banadyga, L. (2026). Antibodies cross-reactive with bundibugyo virus in ferrets vaccinated with ebola virus vaccine. Emerging Infectious Diseases, 32(8) doi:10.3201/eid3208.260948

Title: Multivalent mRNA vaccine platform with compatible antigens conferred broad-spectrum protection against orthoebolaviruses' exposure
Key summary: In this study, a multivalent broad-spectrum mRNA vaccine ([GPs+NP]@LNP) was developed to defend against EBOV, BDBV and SUDV strains. In mouse models, the coordinated antigenic interaction of nucleoproteins and glycoproteins highlights the potential of the vaccine as a promising candidate to protect against all three major pathogenic orthoebolaviruses.
Foci: Vaccination immunity - efficacy/effectiveness; Vaccine immunity measures (Ab, titre, cytokines); Vaccine development
Outcome Summary:
VACCINE IMMUNITY: [GPs+NP]@LNP demonstrated superior immunogenicity compared with a simple mixture of monovalent vaccines. [GPs+NP]@LNP conferred complete, broad-spectrum, and long-lasting protection against lethal orthoebolaviruses infection in animal models, with immunity persisting up to 1 y, suggesting strong potential for clinical application.

VACCINE DEVELOPMENT: Developed a broad-spectrum mRNA vaccine, [GPs+NP]@LNP. It delivers a mix of mRNAs encoding glycoproteins (GPs) from all three viruses plus EBOV nucleoprotein (NP), using a single LNP.
Methods Summary: Developed and evaluated a broad-spectrum mRNA vaccine, designated [GPs+NP]@LNP, formulated with a single lipid nanoparticle (LNP) platform to encapsulate a mixed mRNA payload encoding the glycoproteins (GPs) of EBOV, BDBV, and SUDV, along with the nucleoprotein (NP) of EBOV. This rational antigenic combination was designed to engage both humoral and cellular arms of the immune system.
Citation: Zhang, B., Li, F., Wang, S., Zhou, J., Zhou, J., Wu, Y., . . . Chiu, S. (2026). Lethal VSV-based surrogate mouse models for sudan and bundibugyo ebolaviruses enable antibody evaluation under BSL-2 conditions. Zoological Research, 47(3), 1003â€“1014. doi:10.24272/j.issn.2095-8137.2025.606

Title: Lethal VSV-based surrogate mouse models for Sudan and Bundibugyo ebolaviruses enable antibody evaluation under BSL-2 conditions
Key summary: This study generated recombinant vesicular stomatitis viruses expressing SUDV or BDBV glycoproteins and  established  lethal  infection  models  in  Type  I interferon receptor knockout mice.  Bot viruses induced rapid, dose-dependent mortality; infected mice developed high viral loads in the liver,  spleen, and  lungs,  along with leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and elevated  transaminases  indicating systemic infection and liver injury. Treatment with a single monoclonal  antibody  rEBOV-515   completely protected mice from lethal challenge, reducing viral loads and  restoring  body  weight, establishing reproducible surrogate models for studying  SUDV and BDBV  infection and provide an accessible experimental platform for evaluating GP-targeting therapeutic strategies.
Outcome Summary:
Infection immunity -  MEASURES OF PROTECTIVE antibodies/titres/cytokines):

 In  the  VSV?G-SUDV  GP  cohort,  untreated  mice  exhibited rapid  and  sustained  weight  loss,  succumbing  by  day  4  post infection.  In  contrast,  antibody-treated  animals  showed transient  weight  reduction  followed  by  full  recovery,  resulting in 100% survival . Similarly, infection with VSV?G-BDBV GP induced progressive weight loss beginning at day 2, with  complete  mortality  by  day  7  in  untreated  controls. Administration  of  mAb  515  markedly  attenuated  weight  loss and  conferred  complete  protection,  with  all  treated  mice surviving to the study endpoint. Quantitative  analysis  of  viral  burden  in  major  organs revealed  that  antibody  treatment  prevented  an  increase  in both viral RNA copies and infectious titers. In VSV?G-SUDV GP  infected  mice,  mAb  515  reduced  viral  RNA  loads  by approximately 104.4 copies/g in the liver and 104.2 copies/g in the  lungs,  while  decreasing  live  virus  titers  by  roughly  103.4 TCID50 /g in the liver. Similarly, in VSV?G-BDBV GP infected mice, treatment led to reductions of 103.8 copies/g in the liver and  102.8 copies/g  in  the  spleen,  with  corresponding decreases  of  101.6 TCID50 /g  in  liver  tissue  but  no  significant differences were observed in the brain tissue. These results indicate that mAb 515 prevents or significantly restricts  viral  dissemination  in  target  organs,  effectively curbing viral dissemination and disease progression in IFNAR- / - mice.
Methods Summary: This study used Vero E6 and BSR-T7 cells cultured in DMEM with FBS and antibiotics, and 293F cells in SMM 293-TII medium. A broadly neutralizing antibody, mAb 515, targeting a conserved Ebola virus glycoprotein (GP) epitope, was expressed in 293F cells, purified, and characterized. Recombinant vesicular stomatitis viruses (VSVs) expressing Sudan virus (SUDV) or Bundibugyo virus (BDBV) GP genes were rescued in BSR-T7 cells. Viral growth kinetics were assessed in Vero E6 cells at an MOI of 0.01, with titers quantified using TCID50 assays. Animal studies in IFNAR-/- mice (lacking type I interferon receptors) evaluated viral pathogenicity, antibody efficacy, and systemic infection. LD50 values were determined, and tissues were analyzed for viral load via RT-qPCR and TCID50. Histopathology was performed using H&E and IHC staining, following INHAND guidelines. Statistical analysis used SEM and appropriate tests (t-test, ANOVA).
Citation: Zhang X., Liu X., Zhou J., Gao P., Pan S., Yang X., . . . Yang W. (2026). Computational design of broad-spectrum ebola antibodies through framework and complementarity-determining region synergistic optimization. Research, 9, 1211. doi:10.34133/research.1211

Title: Computational Design of Broad-Spectrum Ebola Antibodies through Framework and Complementarity-Determining Region Synergistic Optimization
Key summary: This study describes a computational–experimental approach for engineering antibodies with stronger and broader activity against diverse Ebola viruses. Modified versions of ADI?15878 and ADI?15946 showed major potency gains—including a 7?fold improvement against BDBV and up to 100?fold against Sudan virus—while retaining stability and cross?virus effectiveness.
Outcome Summary:
VACCINE DEVELOPMENT:
This study presents a computational–experimental pipeline designed to improve the potency and breadth of antibodies targeting highly variable Ebola viruses, including Bundibugyo virus (BDBV).  For ADI?15878, a variant called W32G?LC was created through framework grafting and CDR mutagenesis. This engineered antibody showed significantly enhanced neutralization, including a 7?fold improvement against BDBV, while maintaining structural stability. For ADI?15946, docking-guided multi?site light?chain designs produced variants with dramatically improved activity—up to 100?fold increases against Sudan virus—without reducing effectiveness against EBOV or BDBV.
Methods Summary: This pipeline employs multiple computational tools to prioritize affinity-enhancing mutations in complementarity-determining regions (CDRs) and to explore indirect effects through framework region (FR) modifications. These include mCSM-AB and mmCSM-AB for mutant screening, Foldseek for FR grafting, and ZDOCK for complex modeling. Candidate antibodies were expressed, purified, and evaluated using pseudovirus neutralization assays, flow cytometry binding assays, and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) affinity measurements.
Citation: Zhang, X., Liao, Y., Liu, X., Zhou, J., Gao, P., Dong, X., . . . Yang, W. (2026). Nanobody targeting glycan cap confers broad orthoebolavirus neutralization. Virologica Sinica, 41(2), 413–422. doi:10.1016/j.virs.2026.03.018

Title: Nanobody targeting glycan cap confers broad orthoebolavirus neutralization
Key summary: Identified a high-affinity nanobody, Bundibugyo virus (BDBV)-Nb02 that neutralizes both EBOV and BDBV with high potency and nanomolar affinity and further bivalent engineering enhances neutralization potency by up to 56-fold. Residues F248 and NP278/279 are critical for broad neutralization of Ebolaviruses.
Outcome Summary: PROTEIN INHIBITOR/BINDING INTERACTIONS: Researchers identified a high-affinity nanobody (BDBV-Nb02) from a synthetic phage display library that targets the glycan cap of Bundibugyo virus glycoprotein and potently neutralizes both Bundibugyo and Ebola virus pseudoviruses. Engineering the nanobody into a bivalent format greatly improved neutralization potency by up to 56-fold, while structural analyses showed it targets a conserved glycan cleft, though Fc-fusion reduced its effectiveness by disrupting access to key epitopes.
Methods Summary: Screened a fully synthetic phage display library and identified a nanobody, BDBV-Nb02, which targets the glycan cap region of BDBV GP and characterized its binding kinetics and neutralization activity against EBOV and BDBV pseudoviruses, and demonstrated that a bivalent format markedly enhances its neutralizing potency. Using epitope competition assays, site-directed mutagenesis, and molecular docking analyses, further defined key residues involved in neutralization and elucidated the structural basis underlying its cross-reactive activity.
Citation: Zhou J., Zhang B., Yao Y., Li F., Chen S., Wu Y., . . . Chiu S. (2026). Engineered bispecific antibodies achieve broad and potent protection against multiple ebolavirus species. Emerging Microbes & Infections, , 2668755. doi:10.1080/22221751.2026.2668755

Reference: ZhouJ, et al, (2026), Emerging microbes & infections
Title: Engineered Bispecific Antibodies Achieve Broad and Potent Protection Against Multiple Ebolavirus Species
Key summary: Using both recombinant vesicular stomatitis virus expressing glycoproteins from Ebola virus, Sudan virus, and Bundibugyo virus (rVSV-EBOV/SUDV/BDBV) infection models and authentic mouse-adapted EBOV challenge systems, we demonstrate that these bispecific constructs not only retained the parental neutralization potency but also exhibited markedly enhanced cross-species protection.
Outcome Summary:
VACCINE DEVELOPMENT:
Engineered bispecific antibodies, particularly the IgG?ScFv construct S1+5, markedly enhanced neutralization potency against Bundibugyo virus compared with the parental monoclonal antibodies. While one parental antibody showed almost no BDBV activity, the bispecific designs restored strong binding and reduced PRNT?? values to the low?nanogram range. In  mouse model, S1+5 provided 100% protection against lethal BDBV infection and prevented virus?induced tissue damage. Additionally, S1+5 showed a high barrier to viral escape, maintaining activity across serial BDBV passaging where other antibodies lost effectiveness..
Methods Summary: In this study, we engineered three bsAb formats-CrossMab®, DVD-IgG, and IgG-ScFv-directed against distinct ebolavirus epitopes and systematically characterized their antiviral activities. Evaluated binding and neutralization against EBOV, SUDV, and BDBV using ELISA, recombinant VSV pseudovirus assays, and authentic virus plaque?reduction neutralization tests. Finally, they assessed viral escape through serial passaging under antibody pressure and tested in vivo protection in IFNAR?/? mice challenged with rVSV?EBOV, rVSV?SUDV, or rVSV?BDBV.
Citation: Zhou J., Zhang B., Yao Y., Li F., Chen S., Wu Y., . . . Chiu S. (2026). Engineered bispecific antibodies achieve broad and potent protection against multiple ebolavirus species. Emerging Microbes & Infections, , 2668755. doi:10.1080/22221751.2026.2668755
